Great, thanks! Just a word of warning maybe: Even through at a first glance it seems trivial, usually, porting back to Py2.x requires more work than might be anticipated at first, with four main issues: (1) The command-line interfaces break, (2) file-IO, string, and exception handling has changed, (3) in Py3 essentially everything now is an iterator/generator, and (4) ensuring consistent, correct Unicode support is a pain in certain places with Python 2.x (my main reason why I have stopped using Py 2.x long ago, btw, and never again have seen that pesky `UnicodeError` popping up...). In this case, *probably* only (1) and (4) need to be addressed, but I usually assume the worst case, because that turns out more often than not to be the real case... In any case, if you need help, let me know!

Can you provide an example of a host string that contains a colon (`:`) which
you'd like furl to consider valid?

An example would be helpful because, if I understand you correctly, removing `:`
from `INVALID_DOMAIN_CHARS` would break furl's adherence to URL standards.
Hostnames aren't allowed to contain colons, dating all the way back to the
original [RFC 952](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc952).

```
1. A "name" (Net, Host, Gateway, or Domain name) is a text string up
   to 24 characters drawn from the alphabet (A-Z), digits (0-9), minus
   sign (-), and period (.).
```

[RFC 1123](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1123) expanded on this hostname format to allow the first character of a
domain label to start with a numeric (0..9), but colons remained prohibited.

```
2.1  Host Names and Numbers

   The syntax of a legal Internet host name was specified in RFC-952
   [DNS:4].  One aspect of host name syntax is hereby changed: the
   restriction on the first character is relaxed to allow either a
   letter or a digit.
```

The prohibition of colons in host strings also makes intuitive sense: the first
colon, if present, serves as a delimiter that separates the host from the port
(e.g. `host:port`). If colons are permitted in the host, it becomes ambiguous
whether the token after a `:` is a domain label or a port. For example, if `:`
is allowed to appear in host strings, consider the URL

```
http://host:443/
```

Is `443` a domain label, which can start with a numeric character 0..9, or the
port 443? It's ambiguous.

> I've looked at GDBus, and it looks like they're copying the values while converting between messages and GVariants

Indeed, GDBus isn't zero-copy in this way (but GVariant is, and access to strings that use the GVariant bytestring convention from a DBusMessage can be).

At one point there was a plan for GVariant to be D-Bus message serialization 2.0, although I'm not sure what happened to that.

Thanks everyone, and sorry for not doing anything with this before.

At the end, I don't really have a need for hooks, and most people don't either.

One of the main use cases would be handling environment variables, but that is handled by [pipenv](https://github.com/kennethreitz/pipenv) (a tool that depends on pew) handling of `.env` files

these `.env` files are parsed for the values to set, and thus aren't general purpose hooks. It thus doesn't cover uses cases like shadowing packages installed with `pip install --user`. But I think that using `pip install --user` is not good practice (not much better than `sudo pip install`) and between Nix, virtualenvs and pipsi... people don't have to install packages like that.

Adding hooks would add quite a bit of complexity, and thus I think that keeping things simpler might be for the best.

Seems promising. Is there a known number of bytes that form a header from which pygrib could tell me the level, endStep, etc. of that message?  (I'm not sure if a `pygrib.fromstring()` on just a header with no data would cause it to barf.)  I'm trying to avoid having to download the actual data for every message.

Big picture is that HAS files are giant tars.  Since the tars are uncompressed, we can efficiently build an inventory of its contents (what byte range each grib file is in within the tar) without downloading it (just skipping ahead to the tar headers).

If we could take it a step further and "read" the header bytes of each grib file and determine if we want it, then we wouldn't have to download 250GB of tars to use [5GB] of data.  We'd get reasonably close to random access.

BESST can only work with read data that "looks like" paired reads aligned to contigs, so the only option with BESST would be to construct artificial paired reads from the long reads. There are however scaffolders that are designed to work with long reads (e.g., SSPACE-longread or PBJelly). There are also scaffolders for related species, but I cannot name any right at the top of my head.

Note that it doesn't make sense to have this case fail like it does:

```
def run(*args, **kwargs):
    pass
```

If the programmer wanted the function to fail for no arguments, this is how she'd write it:

```
def run():
    pass
```

The case of accepting args and kwargs is special, and the "pythonic" way of thinking about this function is "this function accepts anything". That's what `argh` should do in this case, too.

Hi,

Yes, this is already possible, but since the documentation is almost non-existent, I'm not surprised you could not find it. I actually took this as an opportunity to write a bit more documentation, so check out https://github.com/olofk/fusesoc/blob/master/doc/fusesoc.adoc#core-search-order where this is described now.

So no more forks of orpsoc-cores. Instead, do one of the following.
If it's a local core (i.e. no [provider] section), copy the directory of the core somewhere else and add the new locations as a core library. Either add the new location to your fusesoc.conf or specify it by running `fusesoc --cores-root=/path/to/directory ...`. You can verify that FuseSoC has picked up the new location by running `fusesoc core-info <your core>` and look at the Core root

If it's a remote core (i.e. with a [provider] section), you must first download the repository specified in the provider section. After that you can copy the .core file from orpsoc-cores into the downloaded repository and remove the [provider] section to avoid FuseSoC from trying to download it again. Some cores already have .core files in their repositories and in those cases, you don't need to copy the one from orpsoc-cores. You now have a local cores and can use the instructions outlined above. Hope this helps and let me know if I can add anything else to the documentation to make this clearer

https://github.com/pavoni/pyvera/blob/master/pyvera/subscribe.py#L71
And then track the state values matching
https://github.com/pavoni/pyvera/blob/master/pyvera/subscribe.py#L13

For most devices I saw a selection of all kinds of states (waiting to start, requeuing...) but ending with a `STATE_JOB_DONE`. If you get that then you can ignore the various intermediate states - and wait for the `done`. At the intermediate states the state of the device is still 'pre-action' - which is why I optimistically set the new state in the vera cache - and then wait for the 'done' state to reset.

I did a bit of work on rst2pdf back in the day.  Originally, all the tests were manual, so when I decided to start contributing -- well, you can blame me for the idea of just automating the functional tests using MD5s rather than writing real unit tests.  One of the reasons I used MD5s was that I didn't particularly want to clutter up the repo with multiple versions of output PDFs.

I still like that basic approach for functional tests, and I'm now using a much simplified version of it in the pdfrw tests (but pdfrw has far fewer external dependencies, so it may actually make more sense there, because I don't have to worry about new versions of reportlab moving things around).

Maybe you could use a hybrid approach for the functional tests where you can log MD5s (fast) and then do the pixel comparison if they mismatch (slow, but then you can update the MD5 database).  If you use the invariant date option on reportlab, you shouldn't wind up with that many MD5s.  Maybe you're already doing that -- I haven't looked at your changes yet.

I don't know how well you know the history, but FWIW:

  - The idea for rson was kicking around in my head for awhile, but I actually developed it after I tried to switch the rst2pdf stylesheets to yaml, and that was really broken and really slow.

  - pdfrw was originally some scripts I was using around the house for my wife's neighborhood newsletter, and I packaged them up specifically for rst2pdf when I got pissed off that none of the previous vector approaches worked very well at all.  I don't know how well svg2rlg works, but it's predecessor sucked, and so did PyPDF (for that application).  Inkscape does a phenomenal job of converting SVG to PDFs, and IMO pdfrw does a fine job of then packaging those up so that reportlab can include them.

Because of the latter, the original tests for pdfrw were basically the vector PDF tests in the rst2pdf toolbox, so I have no problem accepting PRs to make pdfrw work better with rst2pdf -- that was its original reason for existence anyway!

Anyway, good luck with rst2pdf -- it's a big undertaking.  And don't hesitate to ask if I remember any reasons for why I did anything to rst2pdf in any particular way.

Regards,

Systems are developed against specific versions of libraries because they have gone though testing/QA and are a known quantity. If a system needs to be redeployed, then there is a guarantee that it will (or should) behave exactly like the previous deployment because all the dependencies are exactly the same (hence why PyPI doesn't allow replacing files by deleting/re-uploading). By deleting old versions of the ldap3 library we can no longer provide this guarantee that the system will behave the same way as they did in testing/QA. Newer version of libraries my fix bugs, but they also change behaviour that a system may have been relying on.

Users should be allow to choose when to upgrade their dependencies so that they can do proper testing/QA and not have it forced upon them because old version are being deleting. In most cases developers will also find out at the worst possible time that old version no longer exist - ie. trying to redeploy a system as part of disaster recovery. This is definitely not the time that developer what to be dealing with trying to upgrade/QA/test dependency changes.

This basically all boils down to the fact that deleting old version of packages from PyPI is extremely bad practice, and there is generally never a good reason for doing so.

Can you please guarantee that you will *never* delete old packages version from PyPI?

Original comment by: Tarek Ziadé

The unittest issue shouldn't be an issue, as the test suite will use (and implicitly requires) unittest2 to run.

The issue is worse than just the use of with statement, though. Most of the tests use class decorators to skip whole classes when the backend isn't supported. Class decorators are not supported until Python 2.6.

I started work backporting the whole test suite to run under Python 2.5, but found it really made the code ugly. Given that we've made it this far without tests on 2.5, I'm going to mark this as won't fix.

If someone feels strongly about supporting tests on Python 2.5, please prepare a pull request that updates the class decorators to simply wrap the class objects. Make sure the patch is clean (doesn't make other, unrelated changes), so it can be easily backed out at such a time that Python 2.5 is no longer supported.

This is the same problem as in #96. Pretty old one. Each frontend (js/py/rb) binary module is just a thin wrapper around main code.

Function _deleteRuleset_ is defined in

https://github.com/jruizgit/rules/blob/master/src/rules/rete.c#L1977

which in turn calls _deleteBindingsList_ defined in 

https://github.com/jruizgit/rules/blob/master/src/rules/net.c#L2471

Your handle is passed in directly. There are no safeguards, so passing invalid handle just tries to access whatever memory address it is evaluated to and generally SIGSEGVs the process.

import rules
rules.delete_ruleset(1)
```

Oh, one other thought- this only happens on multibody geometry. The `mesh.split` function uses face adjacency which should be robust to these edge cases and by default returns a list of watertight single body meshes. 

Since things are a lot nicer for watertight single body meshes you might consider refactoring your application from:
```
mesh = trimesh.load(file_name)
if not mesh.is_watertight: raise ValueError('oh no')
# do stuff
```
To scene based:
```
loaded = trimesh.load(file_name)

# will create a scene, if loaded is a single mesh it will run mesh.split
# if loaded is a scene it will just return the scene
scene = trimesh.scene.split_scene(loaded)

for name, mesh in scene.geometry.items():
     # split should have given us only watertight results for a single mesh input
     assert mesh.is_watertight

     # do stuff
```

Empty lists, sets, dicts and other containers are specially problematic for ``deep_type``, see https://github.com/python/typing/issues/157. And since pytypes' main way of typechecking is combining deep_type and is_subtype, this can cause trouble. Given that there is not satisfying answer in https://github.com/python/typing/issues/157 we model this with a pytypes-specific ``Empty[T]`` generic.

I can suggest a few ways of ensuring that boto3/botocore won't read credentials from ``~/.aws``:

1. If you export the ``AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`` and ``AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`` environment variables, [botocore.credentials.create_credential_resolver()](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/d0563acbb896bf5127d9e6cde62af2718410d719/botocore/credentials.py#L41) will never move on to loading from files.
2. Since placebo has access to the Session object, it could simply set the ``credentials_file`` and ``config_file`` [session config variables](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/d0563acbb896bf5127d9e6cde62af2718410d719/botocore/credentials.py#L50) to non-existent paths, preventing them from being loaded.
3. In a more generic method, you can simply set ``os.environ['HOME']`` to an empty skeleton directory before running tests, and ``~/`` will expand to that path.

This issue also hits with test suites utilizing VCR that are executed from within a Debian package building process (say during `debuild -us -uc`) since [PyBuild of dh-python uses `http[s]://127.0.0.1:9/` for `http_proxy` and `https_proxy`](https://wiki.debian.org/Python/Pybuild). The precise error is:
```
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '9http'
```

For the next one with this problem where patching VCR is not an option, here's a demo of my current workaround:
```
# Copyright (C) 2017 Sebastian Pipping
# Licensed under CC0 1.0 Public Domain Dedication
from unittest import TestCase

from mock import patch
from vcr_unittest import VCRMixin  # https://github.com/agriffis/vcrpy-unittest

class MyTest(VCRMixin, TestCase):

    # dh-python interferes with VCR, so we need to patch http_proxy away
    # $ fgrep 127.0.0.1:9 /usr/share/dh-python/pybuild
    #         env['http_proxy'] = 'http://127.0.0.1:9/'
    #         env['https_proxy'] = 'https://127.0.0.1:9/'
    _proxy_patcher = patch('six.moves.urllib.request.Request.set_proxy')

    @classmethod
    def setUpClass(cls):
        cls._proxy_patcher.start()
        super(MyTest, cls).setUpClass()

    @classmethod
    def tearDownClass(cls):
        cls._proxy_patcher.stop()
        super(MyTest, cls).tearDownClass()
```

Best, Sebastian

Hello,

this `Pool` implementation was added because there was none which would support disaster recovery and hanging workers while still offering a clean interface. 

Billiard was the closest one but its undocumented and confusing API was causing issues in our systems. Moreover, it was not properly handling timeouts: https://github.com/celery/billiard/issues/104. It might have improved by now.

The implementation is quite stable and we use it in some high load systems.

Among the issues/features it handles/offers:

- timing out operations
- task cancellation (with worker termination)
- crashing workers (python interpreter disasters or C libraries segfaults)
- allows to transfer large data between server and workers
- iteration (`map`) over faulty results

The only known issue I'm investigating (apart from issue https://github.com/noxdafox/pebble/issues/10) is the pool hanging in rare occasions where very large data is being transferred from the workers back to the server at once (in a single result). I'm not 100% sure is due to Pebble though.

The lines you linked deal with hard-killing an unresponsive worker (hanging in a C loop for example) without corrupting the Queue. But there are plenty of other small corner cases to deal with.

"Backspace" can easily be mapped to "delete," since there's no semantic overlap on Mac. Mapping "delete" to "forward delete" is the controversial bit.

I tend to think that "delete" should correspond to the actual "delete" key on Mac; preserving the intuitive (and correct) key names for Mac development seems more useful and friendly than creating a cross-platform mapping that breaks those names.

I'd suggest mapping cross-platform synonyms in the character_to_vk method (as in the PR I updated) rather than just replacing the canonical OS X names. That allows for multiple valid names (as in "command" vs. "windows" or "backspace" vs "delete").

@bmcfee cheers, I've addressed everything I didn't think merits further discussion. I have 3 pending issues, mainly related to validation:

1. Ideally, I'd rather use `util.validate_intervals` over having a function with the same name inside `transcription.py`, however I do think it's good to issue a warning about empty interval sets, so my preference would be to add warnings about empty est/ref intervals to `util.validate_intervals` and then remove `validate_intervals` from `transcription.py` altogether.

2. Currently, `util.validate_intervals` will raise an error `if intervals.ndim != 2 or intervals.shape[1] != 2`. This makes sense for non-empty interval sets, but is perhaps over-strict in the case of empty interval sets that could (for instance) have `intervals.shape == (0,)`? For example an algorithm might initialize a list `intervals = []` to add stuff (e.g. notes) to, but then doesn't detect any, so just returns `np.asarray(intervals)` which will have `intervals.shape == (0,)`, and I'm not sure it makes sense to raise an error because the shape is not `(0,2)`?

Finally

3. Adding an optional `strict=False` parameter to `util.match_events` such that if `strict==True` then the function uses `<` instead of `<=`, and adding another optional parameter `decimals=None` such that if it's not `None` it's an integer indicating how many decimals to round event time differences down to for matching, would make the function more useful, for example it could probably replace [match_note_onsets](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/transcriptionext/mir_eval/transcription.py#L241) which basically implements the aforementioned functionality.

Sure. It's basically which stack frame should trigger the warning. 

```
def foo():
      warnings.warn(stacklevel=2)

foo() #<- this will trigger the warning and is "deprecated"

def qux():
    warnings.warn(stacklevel=3)

qux() #<- won't trigger

def bar():
    qux()

bar() # <- will trigger warning
```

The use if for decorators and other things to mark the code triggering the deprecation as not the current location of warn, but N frames up. It's almost always a stack value of 2 which is the "right" thing to do, but usually you want to couple that with warning filters.

I couldn't try `print(psutil.__version)` because psutil won't allow me to import it. 

I ran a `locate psutil` and found this: 

```
/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/_psutil_osx.so
/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/_psutil_posix.so
```

That is the system python that came with OS X and not the brew installed latest Python. I must have installed an old version of psutil using the old version Python and subsequently uninstalled it but those files were left behind after the uninstall. i prefer [cherrypy style routing](http://tools.cherrypy.org/wiki/RoutesUrlGeneration) , with function names as command. this way we can also provide any additional text as parameters. for example for handling a deep link we can have the following method:
```python
class MyBot(RoutingBot):
    def cmd_start(self):
        self.sender.send("welcome")

    def cmd_start(self, query):
        # do something with deep link query
        self.start()
```

so `/start` will call the first method while `/start abc` when bot is launched from `telegram.me/botname?start=abc` use the second method.

although decorators may be better suited for regex or lambda based routing but i personally prefer this over decorators for normal commands.

---

other than command based routing i also think an extra routing based on last sent keyboard would be nice. so we can define a method to handle keyboard responses and if user sends something from last sent keyboard that method would be called , we should be able to define such method while sending the keyboard to have different methods for each set of keys.

```python
class MyBot(RoutingBot):
    def cmd_start(self):
        # calculate or hardcode some_keyboard
        self.sender.send("welcome", reply_markup=some_keyboard, markup_callback=self.start_keys)

    def start_keys(self, selected_key):
        # do something with the selected_key
```

when `markup_callback` is defined sender method should store keys in a list and check that list in the routing method. of course this would only work in `per_chat_id` and `per_user_id` delegator bots and won't function properly for a `per_application` setup.

alternatively we may use a separate parameter for markup with callback, which would take a list of tuples with `(key_text, key_callback)` to be able to define a separate callback for each key.

```python
#!/home/bor691/pyboz/bin/python
from requests.models import Request
import telepot, json, sys


def handle(update):
    bot = telepot.Bot('TOKEN_HERE')
    if 'message' in update:
        msg = update['message']
        content_type, chat_type, chat_id = telepot.glance(msg)
        if msg['text'] == '/send_file':
            bot.sendPhoto(chat_id, open('./test.png', 'rb'))
        else:
            bot.sendMessage(chat_id, 'Hi')


def patch():
    def _print(url, **kwargs):
        # here i add method to data
        kwargs['data'].update({'method': url.split('/')[-1]})
        # here i fake a request, to leverage requests library and
        # not having to manually create the response body
        r = Request(
            'post',             # method must be post
            'http://anything',  # this doesn't matter as long as it starts with http://
                                # as to not get an exception, we are only interested
                                # in the generated body
            # this is to remove timeout:
            **dict((k, v) for k, v in kwargs.items() if k in ['files', 'data'])
        )
        # create body and headers using requests
        p = r.prepare()

        for header, value in p.headers.items():
            # print headers , since print() can't send bytes i used sys.stdout.buffer
            sys.stdout.buffer.write(':'.join([header, ' ' + value]).encode())
            sys.stdout.buffer.write('\r\n'.encode())
        # the extra linefeed that marks the end of headers
        sys.stdout.buffer.write('\r\n'.encode())
        # requests sends str in case of url-encoded and bytes for multipart
        if isinstance(p.body, str):
            sys.stdout.buffer.write(p.body.encode())
        else:
            sys.stdout.buffer.write(p.body)

    telepot._post = _print


patch()
handle(json.load(sys.stdin))

```

Hello,

You are right it is not the job of this library to check and tell you how to write code, but it IS its job to respect the specification of the document it's trying to generate, which [says about the pattern field](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-fge-json-schema-validation-00#section-5.2.3):

>    5.2.3.2.  Conditions for successful validation
>    A _*string*_ instance is considered valid if the regular expression matches the instance successfully.

The ``raise AssertionError("pattern can only be used when type is string")`` check you are pointing to (and all other sanity checks for types in the ``openapi`` module) is intented to prevent people from directly writing ``openapi.Parameter(type=openapi.TYPE_INTEGER, pattern="blabla")``. It should, barring a bug, never be triggered by the library itself when parsing views and serializers.

Which means that, while in DRF it is valid to have both a ``lookup_field`` and ``lookup_value_regex`` for routing purposes, in OpenAPI it is only valid to apply a ``pattern`` to a ``string`` Parameter or Schema. Which means that it is no mistake of your code, but a bug of this library where it's improperly generating a ``pattern`` for ``integer`` (or any other non-``string``) fields.

While I agree that it is working as intended for the "success" case, what I'm looking for is the ability to more elegantly handle failure, i.e. new functionality. Given that all the consumers I have written (for production at least) are intended to run indefinitely, closing on failure is more common than success.

While I could get around this by committing myself, I'd prefer to use autocommit. There are significant benefits to committing on a regular interval, and I'd prefer not to have to build that functionality again in all my consumers myself given that it's already there.

My understanding of the API's contract is that I process all messages I receive before calling poll() again. As the new consumer is all single threaded, there is no opportunity to commit offsets after poll has returned messages, no matter how much time has passed, until control is passed back. This avoids the issue you describe, so long as you don't try and close().

Note that the issue you describe did exist on the old consumer API, where autocommitting was done in a background thread. This made it essentially unusable in most cases, as it could easily cause messages to be skipped.

@kevmo314 I believe I know what causing the problem.

Line 4 in `templates/xl/worksheets/sheet.xml` is the culprit:
`<sheetView tabSelected="1" workbookViewId="0">`

Using bug.xlsx based on the code given by @acGitUser above, I uncompressed the file and remove `tabSelected="1"` attribute from sheetView tag in `xl/worksheets/sheet2.xml` and compressed back to get the modified xlsx file. As a result, Only one sheet is active, and changing value in sheet 1 does not affect value in sheet 2. 

I think, `tabSelected="1"` is the way to tell Excel which sheets are active. So, if you put them on all sheets, then it means all sheets are active. Hence, changing cell A1 in sheet 2 will affect all sheets.

Therefore, I would suggest two things: Firstly, remove `tabSelected="1"` attribute in the sheet template. And secondly, only apply this attribute to the first sheet after you generate all the sheets. In this way, we only have first sheet that is active.

@bitrut it's wrong. `asyncio.wait` returns awaitable object. You have to wrap to your function like this:

```python

async def main():
    # do something
    pass


async def entrypoint(loop):
    try:
        await main()
    finally:
        await asyncio.wait(t for t in asyncio.Task.all_tasks() if not t.done(), loop=loop)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    try:
        loop.run_until_complete(entrypoint(loop))
    finally:
        loop.close()
```

Every attempted two point conversion passing is a `.receiving_twopta`.  Successfull conversions are a `.receiving_twoptm`.  Unsuccessful conversions are a `.receiving_twoptmissed`.  

I could be wrong, but I believe in all cases the values of `.receiving_twopt[a|m|missed]` mirror `.passing_twopt[a|m|missed]`

```python
import nflgame

games = nflgame.games(2016, week=9, kind='REG')
plays = nflgame.combine_plays(games)

all_rec_attempts = plays.filter(receiving_twopta=True)
for p in all_rec_attempts:
    print p
    print p.receiving_twopta, p.receiving_twoptm, p.receiving_twoptmissed
    print p.passing_twopta, p.passing_twoptm, p.passing_twoptmissed
```

The Json needs to be pruned to be imported, as it contains the source object IDs and other information - so I don't think it makes sense to export the 'raw' object anyway. So this means we'd be doing some form of transformation on the object between the Registry and import/export anyway, so it may as well be useful.
Obviously transforming it from Json to another format introduces some complexity, and will require testing to ensure that no useful information is lost on the round trip. I think this is the same testing that will validate which format is more useful so I'm pushing forward with it.
The main argument for Yaml is that we are moving towards support for things like Ansible, which like to define configurations in Yaml. It makes sense to me to standardise all import/export functions on a single format to reduce code complexity and confusion for the user, so if I can use the same Yaml for import/export that we use for user-defined objects for import, then it's more consistent.
Obviously I need to thoroughly test both options, as at this point I do not know what will work with the API calls, but here's a un-pruned example object in both for you to compare.

Hi @prtome!

The output widget does not differentiate between child and parent rows. So it will output rows as set by the options.

There is a [`output_hiddenColumns` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-output.html#output_hiddencolumns) (`false` by default) which will only include hidden columns if `true` and the column is hidden using `display: none`.

You can make the output widget ignore set columns using the [`output_ignoreColumns` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-output.html#output_ignorecolumns).

And if you want *all* rows to be output, set the [`output_saveRows` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-widget-output.html#output_saverows) to `'a'` (or `'all'`).

If none of those help fix the issue, then please modify [this demo](http://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/abkNM/2617/) and describe the expected output.

I think you mean when `maxApduLengthAccepted` is not defined, and in that case there should be a default value provided in the `LocalDevice.__init__` function.  I added an additional check to make sure the value is at least 50.

The `npdu.npduDADR` contents should be something of type `Address` and the error message says it's a tuple, so rewinding back through the code you set the value to `apdu.pduDestination` so that is also a tuple.  Note also that the `npduDADR` field is only used when the destination is a remote station (specific device on a different network), remote broadcast (all the devices on a different network) or a global broadcast.  So not just any `Address` instance is acceptable.

The fact that you set the `apdu.pduDestination` to a tuple makes me speculate that when the apdu is build the destination is given a socket tuple like ('1.2.3.4', 5), so you could maybe you should build something like `Address(('1.2.3.4', 5))` which converts the tuple to an address object.  To make a remote address you have to do it in two steps:

```
>>> x = Address(('1.2.3.4', 5))
>>> y = RemoteAddress(6, x.addrAddr)
```

And to the other comment question; yes, the hop count always starts out at the maximum value, unless you are doing something special with network paths and routing and you want the "hops" to be clipped short.

The reason is that PySoundFile uses [libsndfile](http://www.mega-nerd.com/libsndfile/), which only allows reading/writing of `short`, `int`, `float`, and `double` ([documentation](http://www.mega-nerd.com/libsndfile/api.html#read)). These are the only data formats that can be read without making one extra copy for data conversion within Numpy.

Since libsndfile 1.0.27 the the new commands `SFC_GET_CUE_COUNT`, `SFC_GET_CUE`, `SFC_SET_CUE` are available, see https://github.com/erikd/libsndfile/commit/9ea30b4c19f56112a10030da7bede2c1c6a0f36d and http://www.mega-nerd.com/libsndfile/command.html.

This might be useful, see also http://stackoverflow.com/q/20011239.

I've ran the example code and I can reproduce the problem. Debugging this shows me that occasionally sessions are "forgotten", i.e. I can get four requests in a row that will start off of the same dictionary of sessions, add theirs. E.g. if the new session IDs are [A, B, C, D] and before those four requests the sessions X and Y existed, I'd end up with [X, Y, A], [X, Y, B], [X, Y, C] and [X, Y, D].

This seems like a race condition in Flask (which may be indicating that I am using the Session API wrong). The issue here is that `DictStorage` is not thread-safe. The only thread_safe storages I would recommend are redis and possibly Postgres. The file-backed storage might work as well, but do not count on it. Do these problems disappear when using Redis?

The TradeAnalyzer uses the "auto"dict feature in which by subclassing a dict and overriding __missing__ (basis for collections.defaultdict) a key can be inserted in real time.

If no trades have been made the keys do obviously not exist.

When a backtesting run comes to an end, the "autodict" is "_closed" which prevents the creation of new keys and therefore avoids potential typing errors. For example for a run with some trades executed and if you had typed:

```python
print(trade_analysis['total']['totol'])
```
and the "autodict" were not yet "_closed" you would actually get an "AutoDict" instance automatically created.

This would be unwanted behaviour, imho, because the typing error is better detected with a "_closed" AutoDict generating an error.

A possible compromise would be to have exactly this value: ``['total']['total']`` filled with a default value of 0, to enable a check in user code.

Unless you have any other suggestion I will add it at once.

btw, here's a better loader than before:

edits to freetype/raw.py:

    import sys,os
    import platform
    from ctypes import *
    import ctypes.util

    from freetype.ft_types import *
    from freetype.ft_enums import *
    from freetype.ft_errors import *
    from freetype.ft_structs import *

    # on windows all ctypes does when checking for the library
    # is to append .dll to the end and look for an exact match
    # within any entry in PATH.
    found = ctypes.util.find_library('freetype')
    filepath,filename = (None,None) if not found else os.path.split(found)

    if filename is None:
        if platform.system() == 'Windows':
            # Check current working directory for dll as ctypes fails to do so
            filepath = os.path.realpath('.')
            filename = 'freetype.dll'
        else:
            filepath = ''
            filename = 'libfreetype.so.6'
        if not os.path.exists(os.path.join(filepath,filename)): filepath=None

        if filepath is None:
            for path in sys.path:
                if os.path.exists(os.path.join(path,filename)): filepath=path; break
                
        if filepath is None:
            raise RuntimeError('Freetype library not found')

    try:
        lastcwd = os.getcwd()
        os.chdir(filepath) # this method succeeds LoadLibrary(os.path.join(filepath,filename)) fails.
        _lib = ctypes.cdll.LoadLibrary(filename)
        os.chdir(lastcwd)
    except (OSError, TypeError):
        _lib = None
        raise RuntimeError('Freetype library dependences not installed')

Doing something along these lines definitely seems reasonable to support. I'm not totally sold on the specific suggestion of doing it at the strategy level (it seems harder than some of the alternatives), but it's definitely neither feasible nor a bad idea. The easiest version is probably to give you an "always save examples which satisfy assumptions in the database" mode to run in.

That being said, I doubt I'll do it soon, so some specific tips for your situation:

Firstly, tests written using Hypothesis are typically a bit longer than their corresponding quickcheck implementations. I'm not sure why but suspect some combo of Python testing culture, a tendency to more non-uniform interfaces to test, and it looking more natural in the language. So you might find it productive to combine some of your tests.

Secondly, in general strategies built up through the standard combinators *shouldn't* be too slow (this isn't really true in that there are a lot of things you can do that end up asking Hypothesis for huge data). So it's quite possible that you've just hit a performance bug. If you show me the strategy you're using (I think I saw an earlier version of it) I can take a look. The most likely culprit is that if you have nested collections you may wish to turn down the size of the interior values (either with an average\_size or max\_size parameter).

This really isn't a `cheat` issue, but pertains to the behavior of the shell in general.

When you run `export` in a shell, the exported variable only persists until the shell is closed. If you want to export a variable in a more "permanent" way, you must do so from within your `~/.profile`, `~/.bash_profile`, or `~/.bashrc` file, depending on the details of how your system is configured. (If you're running a Debian derivative, you'll probably want to modify `~/.bash_profile`.)

This link should get you started:
https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/21598/how-do-i-set-a-user-environment-variable-permanently-not-session

By the way, this was implemented in Python 3.3 (does not exist in Python 2.x): 

`shlex.quote()`

Here is the [Python source](https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/3.4/Lib/shlex.py#l281) for the function: 
```python
import re

_find_unsafe = re.compile(r'[^\w@%+=:,./-]', re.ASCII).search


def quote(s):
    """Return a shell-escaped version of the string *s*."""
    if not s:
        return "''"

    if _find_unsafe(s) is None:
        return s

    # use single quotes, and put single quotes into double quotes
    # the string $'b is then quoted as '$'"'"'b'

    return s.replace("'", "\\'")
```

# coding=utf-8

__author__ = 'Cezary Wagner'

__version__ = '1.1'

import re


class GameVisitor(object):
    def begin_game(self):
        pass

    def end_game(self):
        pass

    def invalid_game(self):
        pass

    def begin_tags(self):
        pass

    def visit_tag(self, tag_name, tag_value):
        pass

    def end_tags(self):
        pass

    def begin_moves(self):
        pass

    def end_moves(self):
        pass

    def visit_move_number(self, number):
        pass

    def visit_move_san(self, move, check, mark):
        pass

    def visit_game_result(self, result):
        pass


class VerboseGameVisitor(GameVisitor):
    def begin_game(self):
        print 'begin_game'

    def end_game(self):
        print 'end_game'

    def begin_tags(self):
        print 'begin_tags'

    def end_tags(self):
        print 'end_tags'

    def begin_moves(self):
        print 'begin_moves'

    def end_moves(self):
        print 'end_moves'

    def visit_move_number(self, number):
        print 'visit_move_number', number

    def visit_move_san(self, move, check, mark):
        print 'visit_move_san', move, check, mark


class Move(object):
    def __init__(self, san):
        self.san = san


class Game(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.moves = []
        self.tags = {}

    def add_tag(self, tag, value):
        self.tags[tag] = value

    def add_move(self, move):
        self.moves.append(move)


class PGNFileError(Exception):
    pass


class PGNFile(object):
    def __init__(self, file):
        self.file = file
        self.end_of_file = False    # end of file reached
        self.chars = ''
        self.line = 0

    def _get_chars(self):
        """ Read buffer of line characters. If empty read next line and memorize. """

        # check if empty
        if not self.chars:
            self.chars = self.file.readline()
            # end of file
            if self.chars:
                self.line += 1
            else:
                self.end_of_file = True

        return self.chars

    def _eat_chars(self, chars):
        """ Eat memorized characters after token parsed. """

        # assert len(chars) <= len(self.chars)

        self.chars = self.chars[len(chars):]


    def _match_and_eat_chars(self, reg_expr):
        match = reg_expr.match(self._get_chars())
        if match:
            self._eat_chars(match.group(0))
            return match
        else:
            return None

    TAG_RE = re.compile(r'''
        \[
        ([A-Za-z0-9]+)
        \s+
        "(.*)"
        \]
        \s*\n
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    EMPTY_LINE_RE = re.compile(r'''
        \s*\n
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    COMMENT_RE = re.compile(r'''
        %.*\n
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    SPACES_RE = re.compile(r'''
        \s+
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    MOVE_NUMBER_RE = re.compile(r'''
        ([1-9][0-9]*)[.]?\s+
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    MOVE_SAN_RE = re.compile(r'''
        (
          (?:           # figure
          [NBKRQ]       # figure letter
          [a-h1-8]?     # ambiguous
          [x]?          # beats
          [a-h][1-8]    # where
          )
          |(?:          # pawn
          [a-h]?        # ambiguous
          [x]?          # beats
          [a-h][1-8]    # where
          (?:=[NBKRQ])? # promotion
          )
          |(?:O-O)      # king side castling
          |(?:O-O-O)    # queen side castling
        )
        (
          \+            # check
          |\#           # checkmate
        )?
        (
          !             # good move
          |!!           # very good move
          |\?           # poor move
          |\?\?         # very poor move
          |!\?          # speculative move
          |\?!          # questionable move
        )?
        \s+
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    MOVE_SAN_SKIP_RE = re.compile(r'''
      (\.{3})()()\s+
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    MOVE_GLYPH_RE = re.compile(r'''
        \$[0-9]+
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    MOVE_VARIATION_BEGIN_RE = re.compile(r'\(')
    MOVE_VARIATION_END_RE = re.compile(r'\)')

    MOVE_COMMENT_BEGIN_RE = re.compile(r'\{')
    MOVE_COMMENT_END_RE = re.compile(r'\}')

    MOVE_GAME_RESULT_RE = re.compile(r'''
        (1-0       # win
        |0-1      # loose
        |1/2-1/2  # draw
        |\*)\s*
    ''', re.VERBOSE)

    def parse_game(self, visitor):
        """ Return next game in file or None if end of file. """

        def on_begin_game():
            visitor.begin_game()

        def on_end_game():
            visitor.end_game()

        def on_begin_tags():
            visitor.begin_tags()

        def on_end_tags():
            visitor.end_tags()

        def on_begin_moves():
            visitor.begin_moves()

        def on_end_moves():
            visitor.end_moves()

        def on_tag(match):
            visitor.visit_tag(match.group(1),
                              match.group(2))

        def on_move_number(match):
            visitor.visit_move_number(match.group(1))

        def on_move(match):
            visitor.visit_move_san(match.group(1),
                                   match.group(2),
                                   match.group(3))

        def on_game_result(match):
            visitor.visit_game_result(match.group(0))

        def before_tag():
            # empty line
            if self._match_and_eat_chars(self.EMPTY_LINE_RE):
                return before_tag()

            # tag
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.TAG_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_game()
                on_begin_tags()
                on_tag(match)
                return after_first_tag

            # comment
            if self._match_and_eat_chars(self.COMMENT_RE):
                return before_tag

            if self.end_of_file:
                return None

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        def after_first_tag():
            # tag
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.TAG_RE)
            if match:
                on_tag(match)
                return after_first_tag

            # empty line
            if self._match_and_eat_chars(self.EMPTY_LINE_RE):
                on_end_tags()
                return after_tags

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        def after_tags():
            # move number
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_NUMBER_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_moves()
                on_move_number(match)
                return after_first_move_number

            # move san
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_SAN_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_moves()
                on_move(match)
                return after_white_move_san

            # move white skip san
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_SAN_SKIP_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_moves()
                on_move(match)
                return after_white_move_san

            # move game result
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_GAME_RESULT_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_moves()
                on_game_result(match)
                return after_game_result

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        def after_first_move_number():
            # move san
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_SAN_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_moves()
                on_move(match)
                return after_white_move_san

            # move white skip san
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_SAN_SKIP_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_moves()
                on_move(match)
                return after_white_move_san

            # move game result
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_GAME_RESULT_RE)
            if match:
                on_begin_moves()
                on_game_result(match)
                return after_game_result

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        def after_move_number():
            # move san
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_SAN_RE)
            if match:
                on_move(match)
                return after_white_move_san

            # move game result
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_GAME_RESULT_RE)
            if match:
                on_game_result(match)
                return after_game_result

            # space at start of line
            if self._match_and_eat_chars(self.SPACES_RE):
                return after_move_number()

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        def after_white_move_san():
            # move san
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_SAN_RE)
            if match:
                on_move(match)
                return after_black_move_san

            # move game result
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_GAME_RESULT_RE)
            if match:
                on_game_result(match)
                return after_game_result

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        def after_black_move_san():
            # move number
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_NUMBER_RE)
            if match:
                on_move_number(match)
                return after_move_number

            # move san
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_SAN_RE)
            if match:
                on_move(match)
                return after_white_move_san

            # move game result
            match = self._match_and_eat_chars(self.MOVE_GAME_RESULT_RE)
            if match:
                on_game_result(match)
                return after_game_result

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        def after_game_result():
            # empty line
            if self._match_and_eat_chars(self.EMPTY_LINE_RE):
                on_end_moves()
                on_end_game()
                return None

            raise PGNFileError('Error in line %s: %s', self.line, self._get_chars())

        state = before_tag
        while state:
            state = state()

        return True
```

#59 resolved the issue with cookies not being associated with response objects. That appears to work now, but only for simple cookies that themselves do not have commas within them as the assumption is in the `set-cookie` header multiple cookies are separated by commas.

The problem stems from the requests library which simply concatenates multiple `set-cookie` headers into a single `requests.structures.CaseInsensitiveDict` thus making it very difficult to reliably separate distinct cookie fields.

The solution I am working toward is to separately serialize the `response.cookies` field. For backwards compatibility `cookies` should only be serialized if it is non-empty, and the `set-cookies` header should continue to be output as-is when it's present. Thus, upon deserializing the cookies will be initial set from the `set-cookie` header if present and actually parseable (handling the backwards compatible case) and only overwritten when `cookies` is explicitly serialized in the cassette.

After a bit of thinking my decision is to define enums describing the "none policy", to be used as parameters, and with the following behaviours:

 * for inline validation (`is_valid`, `assert_valid` and `Validator`), by default `None` is treated as any other variable and enters the validation procedure. This corresponds to the default parameter value `none_policy=NonePolicy.VALIDATE`. Users may override this by setting `none_policy=NonePolicy.FAIL` or `none_policy=NonePolicy.SKIP`, or by explicitly using the provided `is_not_none` validation function in their list
 * for function input validation, by default the function signature is inspected to check if the argument is Nonable, meaning that it either presents a default value of None, or has a PEP484 type hint containing `Optional` or `NoneType` / `type(None)`, even in nested `Union`s. If so, the `none_policy` is set to `SKIP`, otherwise to `VALIDATE` (None are sent to the validators). This behaviour corresponds to the default parameter value `none_policy=NoneArgPolicy.SKIP_IF_NONABLE_ELSE_VALIDATE`. Users may override this by setting any of the policies described above, or the additional `none_policy=NoneArgPolicy.SKIP_IF_NONABLE_ELSE_FAIL` to fail on None when not explicitly stated in the signature.

Hopefully this will provide consistency and intuitive behaviours (crossing fingers here :) ).

For a dynamic MUX type architecture where a new language kernel is instantiated and can directly hook with the server over ZMQ for a websocket bridge, i think IPython may be very interesting to look at. 

I played around with IPython and was able to interact with it in an App/Analyses context. Here is a simple [application] (https://github.com/minrk/singlecell) created by an IPython author. Some thoughts:
- On the backend, IPython uses tornado to create ZMQ/Websocket bridges. For a minimal hook into IPython, we may want to use use Tornado instead of Gevent. (http://ipython.org/ipython-doc/2/development/messaging.html). However, Flask could still be used for auth/routing and templating. More reading on [IPython messaging]
- For front-end, current databench.js will have to be modified to work with ipython kernel machinery
- The current ws_serve and zmq bridges will need to be factored out to create to fork new kernels and manage them. 
- Furthermore, there are security concerns with exposing IPython kernels on the web - perhaps docker or other VM are needed
All in all this calls is a significant architecture change and i am not sure if it fits into the vision of what you are trying to accomplish. One benefit of using IPython is that they are moving to a polyglot architecture with many different language kernels already implemented e.g. Julia, R etc. Let me know your thoughts. I will add a simple working demo using IPython soon.

> Is it somehow possible to inject my locals into aioconsole.start_interactive_server()?

It is, using the `factory` argument: 

```python
   factory = lambda streams=None: \
        aioconsole.AsynchronousConsole(
            locals={'a': 1}, streams=streams)
    server = await aioconsole.start_interactive_server(
        factory=factory,
        host='localhost',
        port=8000)
```

The `start_interactive_server` coroutine does not have a `locals` argument because it might not make sense in the context of another factory (such as `AsynchronousCli`).

Another way to achieve the same purpose is to use `InteractiveEventLoop`

```python
loop = aioconsole.InteractiveEventLoop(locals={'a': 1}, serve=('localhost', 8000))
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
```

Maybe we should add some kind of helper (e.g. `start_console_server`) that would fit your use case in a better way.

Also, note that the locals dictionary is going to be updated at runtime.

I found the issue @vxbinaca.

You were right that this was an issue with `--delete`, and also with `--checksum` (`--delete` sets `--checksum` to True).

The issue was that when a file already exists in the item (based on checksum), no request is sent and an empty `requests.Response()` object is returned. For some reason calling `r.ok` on an empty Response object works just fine in PY2, but not PY3.

No, no  - good question :-) ... you can still update individual pixels - Basically, any of the methods on [ImageDraw](https://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/4.0.x/reference/ImageDraw.html) are valid inside the canvas context, so you can do stuff like:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
# Copyright (c) 2014-17 Richard Hull and contributors
# See LICENSE.rst for details.
# PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK

"""
Draw random dots
"""

import time
import random
from demo_opts import device
from luma.core.render import canvas


def main():
    while True:
        with canvas(device) as draw:
            for i in range(4):
                x = random.randint(0, device.width)
                y = random.randint(0, device.height)

                # 'draw' is an ImageDraw object.
                draw.point((x, y), fill="white")
                time.sleep(0.05)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
```

For the LED matrix, any fill color (except "black") will switch the led on.

def _may_retry_on_error(r, protocol, wait):
    # The genericerrorpage.htm/internalerror.asp is ridiculous behaviour for random outages. Redirect to
    # '/internalsite/internalerror.asp' or '/internalsite/initparams.aspx' is caused by e.g. TLS certificate
    # f*ckups on the Exchange server.
    if (r.status_code == 401) \
            or (r.headers.get('connection') == 'close') \
            or (r.status_code == 302 and r.headers.get('location', '').lower() ==
                '/ews/genericerrorpage.htm?aspxerrorpath=/ews/exchange.asmx') \
            or (r.status_code == 503):
        if r.status_code not in (301, 302, 401, 503):
            # Don't retry if we didn't get a status code that we can hope to recover from
            return False
        if protocol.credentials.fail_fast:
            return False
        if wait > protocol.credentials.max_wait:
            # We lost patience. Session is cleaned up in outer loop
            raise RateLimitError('URL %s: Max timeout reached' % r.url)
        return True
    return False
```

Hey @cosmologist10, you should be looking at the api here: https://django-reversion.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api.html#creating-revisions.

Now where to put this really depends on the app architecture, you can either override `save()` method of form used to save the model or you can override the same method of model class itself. 

In short, you need wrap the save() method, so reversion know what has changed before saving the model instance.

If you create models in your own business views, use `RevisionMiddleware` to create the revisions, or even better, `reversion.create_revision()` decorator/context manager.

The trouble with this patch is that it will prevent fast-path deletion in Django (which is a lot faster!), and result in more revisions being saved in the usual case where models have a revision created on first save.

You did not mention `virtualenv`, so I assume you are trying to install this package system-wide as `root`. I do ***not*** recommend this, but it should still work.

I guess this sums up the commands you ran:
```sh
$ git clone https://github.com/remcohaszing/pywakeonlan.git
$ cd pywakeonlan
$ ./setup.py build  # This is redundant.
$ ./setup.py install
```

Note that the shebang in `setup.py` refers to `/usr/bin/env python`. If I'm correct this refers to Python 2.7 on your system.
```sh
$ /usr/bin/env python --version
```
This is why it gets installed as a Python 2.7 package.

The closest to your current solution is to explicitly call `setup.py` with the preferred Python version.
```sh
$ python3 setup.py install
```

However, I recommend installing it using `pip`
```sh
$ pip3 install wakeonlan
```

PATH_INFO is the path within your application and SCRIPT_NAME is the path to the root of your application. Both together can be used to generate external URLs. In general SCRIPT_NAME is empty and PATH and PATH_INFO have the same value. However if you expose your application behind a reverse proxy and let's say you proxy a sub path (e.g. /myapp) to your application, then either the WSGI server or some middleware like repoze.vhm or similar can be used to set up the SCRIPT_NAME and PATH_INFO variables accordingly. E.g. a request to /mayapp/api/call should produce a SCRIPT_NAME=/myapp and PATH_INFO=/api/call (PATH would be the full thing /mapp/api/call). In this scenario request.path won't match any of the swagger api paths as it contains /myapp, but request.path_info will match, as it is relative to your app root.

How SCRIPT_NAME and PATH_INFO is generated really depends on your WSGI server and/or middle ware you use. UWSGI allows you to use mount points, there is also paste.deploy.config.PrefixMiddleware and my personally preferred way uses repoze.vhm (no configuration needed in the app deployment config)

Pyramid's request methods to generate URLs (like request.route_url etc...) are aware of these environment variables and will produce correct external URLs.

Pythran does not support returning different types. If you write this

```python
def foo(n):
    if n:
        return 1
    else:
        return "1"
```

Then this code is not considered as statically typable, as the return
type depends on runtime value of ``n``.

Is that your situation?

I'd try one of two things:

1) If possible, make 'Geo' the default schema for the relevant user. I haven't 
actually played with SQL Server in a long time, and if this suggestion makes 
no sense, please ignore it.

2) add to your model a Meta class setting the table name:
```
	class Asset(models.Model):

		class Meta:

			db_table = '"Geo"."Asset"'
```
or
```
			db_table = 'Geo"."Asset'
```

The first is supposed to work because the backend is not supposed to mess with 
a name if it starts and ends with double quotes. The second might also work. 
plain `'Geo.Asset'` will not work, because the backend adds quotes when they 
are not present.

True, there is not a way to customize all the message keys, but I do wonder what value that really brings to the extension. If you are just passing back the default error messages already, in the majority of cases the frontend and backend will need to agree on what the structure for how those error messages looks like already, so just changing the key and no other structure of the data doesn't make much sense to me. The frontend should be able to parse the JSON result and grab the message string and display it however it wants, without any changes needing to happen on the backend. 

On the flip side, there is already a built in way to change the error messages to however you want built in to the application. By adding this additional way to change only the key, my worry is that a new user might be more confused on how to customize their errors (if they only saw this new key option and not the decorators that let them fully customize their error responses, for example).

Your PR lacks important information and possibly introduces a critical bug. I don't think such PR would be accepted in any project.

> Indeed, I'm using whatever was the default in the latest version of Django.

This is not true. Django uses `StaticFilesStorage` by default: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/settings/#std:setting-STATICFILES_STORAGE
This storage class does not use hash versioning of static files or `manifest.json` and you shouldn't have had any troubles running `collectstatic` with default-generated `settings.py`.

>For reference, the original django-tinymce does this:

They (and you) use naive approach with hardcoded `JS_URL`. This will not work with static files Storage classes that use hash versioning. Hello,

I believe the issue comes from the magic behind: `ordering = ('name',)`

It will get automatically applied to the translation aware manager, which, unfortunately does not handle it well. As it's not used anyway, you can override it using an `order_by`.
Better yet, drop the `ordering = `, because applying it in *every* query usually does not make sense and incurs additional load on the database — adding an explicit `.order_by()` to the querysets as needed is a better approach.

By the way, you don't need a translation aware manager for this request. This should work (or not work, until you remove the ordering) just the same:
```python
kwargs['related_objects'] = KnowledgeElement.objects.filter(operating_level=self.oplevel)
```
As a rule, keep uses of translation aware manager to the bare minimum. It is a very old piece of hvad, that has not had the new features added and is generally much less used, so more likely to have bugs. Most requests you would use a translation aware manager for usually can be expressed with subqueries.

Now, as you're using `topic` and `topic.name` in your template, I strongly encourage you to explicitly prefetch them, because otherwise, each topic will generate an additional query for the topic itself, plus yet another additional query for the translation.

You would do this that way:
```python
from django.db.models import Prefetch

kwargs['related_objects'] = KnowledgeElement.objects.filter(operating_level=self.oplevel).prefetch_related(
    Prefetch('topic', queryset=Topic.objects.language())
)
```
It would be better with a `select_related`, but Django does not yet support an explicit queryset for this one. Still, 2 queries is better than 1 + 2n.

You might also want to filter out topics which are not translated in current language. Unfortunately, *hvad* does not provide a clean abstraction for this right now, but it's easy to write manually:

```python
kwargs['related_objects'] = KnowledgeElement.objects.filter(
    operating_level=self.oplevel,
    topic__translations__language_code=get_language(),   # only keep items whose topic is available in current language
).prefetch_related(
    Prefetch('topic', queryset=Topic.objects.language())
)
```

I hope it helps.

After thinking about this more, the plan moving forward is like so:

1. `pymdownx.extra` will be like the default `extra` but will enhance `fenced_code` and `smartstrong` with `superfences` and `betterem` respectively.  `pymdownx.extra` will replace `extra`.

2. `pymdownx.extra` will contain additional profiles: `pymdownx` and `github`.  `pymdownx` will be extra +  and the rest of the additional (non special case) extensions.  `github` will be extra + additional extensions configured in such a way to give a github flavored markdown feel.

3. Both `pymdownx.pymdown` and `pymdownx.github` will be removed.

If you want to mix and match or configure individual plugins, you will have to break them apart and include them individually.

`smartstrong` should **not** be used with `betterem`!
 `fencedcode` should **not** be used with `superfences`!
`extra` should **not** be used with `pymdown.extra`!

Additionally the processing of raw html (which was originally part of extra) will be split out to be included individually for those who want to have this feature, but still assemble extensions manually.

solution 1) in web2py change Field('record_id', 'integer') into Field('record_id', 'string') for MongoDB only
solution 2) in web2py change Field('record_id', 'integer') into Field('record_id', db._adapter.gen_ref_type') where gen_ref_type (or other name) is defined in pydal by the adapter and can be string or integer.
solution 3) introduce a new type 'generic-reference' (as opposed to 'reference something') and use  Field('record_id', 'generic-reference')

1 is easier but I think 3 would be best.

Why not use a json type for list:integer or list:string? It doesn't make much sense to provide a generic form like 'list:xxx' to take advantage of various NoSQL options' native list types, but exclude lists of types that are supported by those same NoSQL flavors that would be easy to implement in SQL. web2py already allows comparisons on regular float/double columns, e.g. `db.foo.some_float == 3.14159` or `db.foo.some_float >= 1.0`. I don't see that this is really any different, or that it encourages bad practice (my `testfl.contains()` above was just an example to show that the list:double type worked).

If you're still against it, though, it might at least be worth a sentence in The web2py Book explaining that 'list:decimal' and 'list:float' will not be implemented in the DAL because of the rounding issues inherent to floating point calculations, and that a user should use json instead.

Personally, for my own use case (list of credit values supported by a slot machine), I think a list:decimal will work better anyway, and I'll work on adding that in my branch.

What we can do is:
- Add imperative support for `nd.where`. symbolic execution is not supported. To do so, we just need to rename the backend operator and add wrappers in the ndarray/symbol frontend, where `ndarray.where` supports `x=None` and `y=None`. It's hard to support symbolic execution since we have to block and wait on the completion of such operation, which makes shape inference of the rest of the operators in the graph very hard. 
- Internally, the `_where(cond)` operator will register `FComputeEx`, and output `row_sparse` output. The size of the output is `[n, num_dims]`, where `n` is the number of total elements in `cond`, `num_dims` is the number of dimension of `cond`. At runtime, the operator will generate data based on the number of true elements in `cond`. To know how many true elements are there, we just need to access `output.data`. 
- Interface-wise, we should not return the row_sparse output to user directly. Instead, the wrapper first call `_where(cond)` to get the row_sparse output, and access `output.data` to get the indices. In order to make it easier for users to access these elements using advanced indexing, we can use `split` or `take` to return a list/tuple of **1-D** indices. We might need to loop through all elements to make sure they are 1-D. 

\* currently advanced indexing only takes tuple of ndarrays as input indices. @reminisce it will be good to confirm if we can turn on the support of ndarray list easily (if there's no ambiguity). 
\* this is more like an add-hot solution for `where`. If there are more general use cases like this, we should bring them up and discuss a general solution (like the dynamic shape in tf).

So there is a little bit of misunderstanding there it seems. The fix to the bug in NCCL 2.1 you are referring to will not make this test work in p2 instance with 16 GPUs (even though the actual NCCL integration works well in that scenario). It should only make the test fail gracefully instead of segfaulting.

There is a limit to the number of P2P peers any given GPU may have (it is a hardware limit for all PCI-E hardware, not just GPUs) - it is 8. NCCL under normal circumstances works fine with p2 instances with 16 GPUs under the same PCI-E tree because it uses a ring for communication, so each GPU establishes P2P access only between its neighbors in a ring.

So why is the test broken then? Because (being a test) it does a sweep over multiple configurations (in this case ring sizes), going from 1 to N (= number of GPUs in a node). During this sweep it produces rings as follows:
0
0 - 1 (gpu 0 opens P2P connection to GPU 1)
0 - 1 - 2 (gpu 0 opens P2P connection to GPU 1 and 2 - it is a ring)
0 - 1 - 2 - 3 (gpu 0 opens P2P connection to GPU 1 and 3)
...
As you can see, during the execution of the for loop over test cases, GPU 0 opens P2P connections to GPUs 1,2,3,... and at some point reaches its limit of 8 peers (during ring 1 - .... - 9) and then fails during the 1 - ... - 10 ring test case. This is expected behavior (unfortunately). 

To fully fix this test (which you can enable right now, btw, you don't need to wait for the next version of NCCL to do that) you would need to either:
 - split the test into 2 very similar tests - 1 covering range from 1 to 8, and second from 9 to 16 (which is very simple, just copy the test_nccl.py file and in the second copy remove limiter on `num_gpus` and do `gpus=range(9, 1 + num_gpus)` instead of `gpus=range(1, 1 + num_gpus)`)
 - make the test choose gpus assignment to ring smarter, like this (with 16 gpus):
0
0 - 1
15 - 0 - 1
15 - 0 - 1 - 2
14 - 15 - 0 - 1 - 2
...
which would ensure that each gpu opens only up to 4 p2p connections.

WebHDFS is not required, but to use `from_pandas`, you have to specify either `in_query` or `webhdfs` as the method to transport the data.  The former actually creates a DDL statement with the dataframe hardcoded into the SQL query as literals, which may be bad if you have a lot of data.  So while WebHDFS is not required, I anticipate it'll be better for larger data frames.

I actually thought about doing exactly this but came down against it because I think this default would make it too easy to mask programming errors. I would argue that even in general it's _usually_ a bad idea to blanket catch `Exception` in python for this reason. For backoff in particular, if you don't configure a logger it would be very easy to completely hide a simple bug, think `KeyError` or `AttributeError`.

Hopefully there would normally be a more specific exception you could target, even if it's an abstract one. For example with [requests](http://docs.python-requests.org) all relevant exceptions inherit from `requests.exceptions.RequestsException`.

If you have an exception (pun unavoidable) to what I would consider the best practice of not catching `Exception` in python, then I think it's fair to explicitly have to pass `Exception` to `backoff.on_exception`.

Does this make sense to you? I'm glad you're choosing backoff and thanks for the feedback.

Nigel, thank you very much for that brief chat, it was incredibly useful.

For the "record" :), the behaviour we are encountering here is reasonable given the way information is passed back and forth between the server and client (which can reside on entirely different end points within a network).

Specifically, the server does not inform the client on every little change of state, *except*,  when specific actions are carried out. 

This is why, in our case, the exception might be taking place within the `run`, but we only find out during the retrieval of the results. 

Synchronising state for all actions can be costly and this is why it is avoided. We can however trigger a synchronisation via the `sync` method of the `session` object and this is probably the best option here, now that we know that we might run into this problem.

Good point. And what you've raised is part of why it's important that it be possible to select different loaders. Overriding `parse_command_line` has always been possible, but that would require more copy/paste than should be necessary.

> what you suggest is to change the default loader, or drop and remove code from #322 completely?

I'd rather not revert it. It's an improvement to a problem we've had for a long time: no good way to expose container traits on the command-line. It's just not quite the complete solution we need. There are three cases: `--short` aliases, `--Class.trait` in the classes list, and `--Class.trait` *not* in the classes list. I think it would be better to either:

1. confine it to `--flag` short aliases, or
2. Find a way to extend it to `--Class.trait` where Class is not in the config list.

Either way, I think it's important to remove the distinction case 2 and 3.

Now, `from_string` makes this easy as long as args can only be one string. But as you pointed out, letting the shell split items is nicer than that. Maybe there's something like an `item_from_string` approach that will let us keep the improvements you've made while simplifying things for `--Class.trait` to defer to the traits more.

Hi Pyry,

If you look over here:
https://cloud.google.com/dataproc/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.regions.jobs#SparkJob

You'll see that there's 2 ways to get your main class to spark. One is to submit a normal jar using "jarFileUris" and then tell spark which class in your jar file to run using the "mainClass" parameter. The other one is to build a jar file that has some extra meta info that specifies it's own main class. If you go that way you can submit it with "mainJarFileUri".

This api has been in place since the v1 release of Dataproc and hasn't changed since then. The current Luigi code only supports the way of submitting jar files and the mainClass separately. (And it very much works because I'm using it.) If you'd like to make a pull request that adds the functionality to use the "mainJarFileUri" way of submitting jobs (along with an extra test case) that'd be very useful, however the pull request as is breaks the existing functionality of submitting jars and the main class separately.

Thanks,
Constantijn

P.S.
If you submit your current jar using "jarFileUris" and then also fill in the "mainClass" parameter to pint at the fully qualified name of your main class i believe it should also work with the current Luigi code and your current jar file.

Thanks for the PR, but I think a better direction to go here would be to remove the indefinite article "a". Making a change to attempt to choose the correct indefinite article adds complexity that doesn't actually make the documentation easier to understand. Further, using "a" vs "an" based only on if the first letter is "A" (or even if the first letter is a vowel) [is inadequate](http://www.grammar.com/a-vs-an-when-to-use/), and trying to account for all of the cases we'd need to make this accurate would have a low return on investment. For example, we'd need to know the difference between "a horse" and "an hour". I don't think we can reasonably choose the correct indefinite article, and therefore should just remove the indefinite article altogether.

So "A/an %s resource" should become "%s resource". Could you update the PR to just remove the indefinite article?

[![Coverage Status](https://coveralls.io/builds/2195208/badge)](https://coveralls.io/builds/2195208)

Coverage decreased (-0.03%) to 95.61% when pulling **8d5176d3c3c3f4a64d1693805876c3f161ebdd32 on kyleknap:endpoint-remove-region** into **16c8f69fb3be1b75734ba767098d5ea2bb077f14 on boto:clients-only**.

This isn't something we expect users to set since the Content-MD5 is that of the whole request, so instead we [set it for them](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/handlers.py#L172). * So we can properly match it with the last update from that source (not yet there, but will be needed).
* Tracking purposes, as it's not the same  coming from a spider that crawls directly publisher A, than coming from desy, even if both originally came from the same publisher A.

But yes, having it in the metadata somehow might be enough, just proposed the directory structure for easy organization and implementation (50 dirs is not that many, and easily allows seeing if any provider source is empty or not being crawled properly, adding it to the metadata only means having to check the contents of the files every time you want to know something similar).

The key point being, we need a stable and reliable way of knowing the origin of the record.
