To prove the reusability of my approach, I made another iteration into branch 

    https://github.com/collective/sphinxcontrib-httpexample/tree/setup-nix

And exported reusable Nix expressions into

    https://github.com/datakurre/setup.nix

Next I'm going to write README for setup.nix project.

With this iteration python package repository only needs to have ``setup.nix`` and ``requirements.nix``. ``requirements.nix`` is the file generated and maintained using ``pip2nix``. ``setup.nix`` includes overrides and calls ``setup.nix``-project to make all ``setup.nix``-derivations available:

* ``nix-build setup.nix -A build`` builds the package with minimal ``buildInputs``

* ``nix-shell setup.nix -A develop`` enters package development shell with all packages in ``requirements.nix`` in its ``buildInputs``

* ``nix-build setup.nix -A env`` builds python interpreter with all package in ``requirements.nix`` (but not the package itself)

* ``nix-build setup.nix -A install`` builds python interpreter only with the package itself

* ``nix-build setup.nix -A sdist`` builds sdist for the package

* ``nix-build setup.nix -A bdist_wheel`` builds wheel for the package

* ``nix-shell setup.nix -A pip2nix --run "pip2nix generate -r requirements.txt --output=requirements.nix"`` updates requirements.nix

Naming is hard. I tried to follow existing names from normal setup.py commands. ``env`` is just ``env``, because Nix-created env is not really ``venv``.

In addition setup.nix also exposes a version of ``pythonPackages`` with all packages in ``requirements.nix``.

Those are possible paths forward.

No, no pun intended.
And no, you cannot plug smmap back, it is not ready.

But I will write down some conclusions, for my efforts not to go in vain:

1. in PY3 (and *Windows* more so) the main problem is rogue `memoryview` instantiations, preventing underlying `mmap` instances to close (see this [QnD notebook](https://gist.github.com/ankostis/91985fc1a7a33b964eeae9f198e8faee#file-smmap_scratch-ipynb)).

2. Resource management coupled with non-deterministic destructors is not working - either you have to be strict: only one entrance/exit allowed, to debug missuses, or you have to waste hours to build flexible APIs that work both as context-managers and with destructors, and eventually...break!

3. Resource accounting is easier when centralized ala DB, i.e., having only immutable handles (regions and cursors) delegating associations to the central "manager" who accomplishes that with "indexes" (enhanced dictionaries).  In case of errors, you can easily inspect those "indexes" to detect irregularities, than inspecting dispersed instances.

4. Hand;es (regions & cursors) are easier when immutable and implemented with a very simple context-management behavior: fire once, and then die.  

5. Maintaining PY2.6, PY3.3 is heavily taxing this project.

6. I'm not sure it is easy to provide a [sound API for a tiling memory-manager & sliding cursors](https://github.com/ankostis/smmap/blob/leaks_memview/doc/source/tutorial.rst#design).

> Is there something that pipenv relies on that is flaky?

Everything is intended to be as deterministic as possible. There are a *lot* of factors involved — ranging from your local pip cache, to the pipenv cache, to the lockfile needing to be re-created, to the availability of PyPI (etc), to installation differences, etc. 

> Should I uninstall my whole brew environment and recreate it?

No — I'd just either 'brew install pipenv' or 'pip3 install pipenv'. 

> Should I be using a specific stable version of pipenv rather than whatever becomes available via pip3 install pipenv?

Each release is intended to be more stable than the last. That's the goal :)

> What would cause pipenv to decide that secondary dependencies are blank?

This shouldn't be happening. Ensure you're using the latest version.

> What would cause dependencies could not be resolved/Could not find a version that matches" errors?

Mis-matching dependencies — one thing requires requests==2, another <2.

> What would cause Pipfile.lock files to sometimes not get created?

That sounds like a legitimate bug, unless an error was raised.

That's right. `setup.py install` invokes the egg protocol which was famously deprecated. I'd say it's unsupported to support mixed-method environments. It's difficult enough trying to support namespace packages in just one or the other modes.

First and foremost, I want to support `pip install .`, `pip install -e .`. Secondarily, I want to support pure non-pip installs through `setup.py install` and `setup.py develop`, although I consider that model (soft) deprecated and will eventually be superseded by `pip` entirely.

There's a proposal to actually replace `easy_install` with `pip install` in #917, which if that's viable would eliminate these differences.

I welcome others to dive in and work on #917 or otherwise try to make these modes work together, but to what advantage? Can you see any reason not to cut our losses and focus on the future?

@jni Hey there! I knew I shouldn't have opened my mouth without being better prepared :)

Anyhow, my main point is that there are (at least) three separate concepts that all too often get conflated when discussing array indexing, particular in the context of images:

1. Layout of multidimensional data on disk / in file formats.
2. Layout of multidimensional data in memory, especially as it relates to cache-optimal image-iteration strategies.
3. Logical / display ordering of the indices with regard to each dimension.

So, when you talk about the Leica Image Format, that's really a case of concept 1, while discussion about array ordering (as in the skimage docs) is a case of concept 2. But why do those docs talk about what order the axes are _labeled_ in (concept 3) as if it has anything to do with performance? 

As long as you iterate over an image in order of fastest to slowest moving index (in memory), you get good performance regardless of how the numpy strides tuple is configured. A lot of people (not you, @jni, but many who should know better) don't seem to get this. **But arbitrarily strided arrays are one of the particular bits that make numpy really great to use!!** Moreover, numpy's C API has various nd iterator tools, so that one can iterate over an arbitrary numpy array in a memory-efficient fashion.

And in particular, numpy striding lets users break free from the tyrannical connection between concepts 2 and 3. Using striding, you can let an array be laid out in a a memory-optimal fashion while indexing it in whatever way is most appropriate to a given application. If an algorithm is smart about iteration (i.e. either using numpy's C iterators, or doing a bit of inspection on the stride array), then you can just pass your array to the algorithm and all is well. If the algorithm makes assumptions about memory layout matching the index ordering, then it's almost as easy: a quick transpose operation (or otherwise rearranging the axis order without actually touching the memory), and then you can pass off your numpy array to the algorithm.

So I think it's reasonable to state that many microscopy folks would generally like to logically index their data as `(x, y, z, c, t)` (which is more or less how ImageJ and the OME Data Model do it), but would prefer memory layouts to look like (slow to fast moving) `TZYXC` or `TZCYX`, depending on how the color data is acquired and used.

And if there are use-cases where it makes more sense to say `arr[z]` rather than `arr[:,:,z]`, then let then use `(z,x,y,c,t)` or whatever. Let a thousand flowers bloom! Numpy striding allows it!

> Further, row-col coordinates allow direct indexing into a NumPy arrays. When we were a mix of xy and rc, we had to decide whether a function would take NumPy coordinates or "real" coordinates. Now they are equivalent. That's a massive benefit. (And note that this is also true of SciPy ndimage: the coordinate order in e.g. map_coordinates corresponds to the axis order in the array.)

Here we are in 100% agreement. Array index ordering should be treated as authoritative. There should never be a distinction between "numpy coordinates" and "real coordinates". If you index `array[10, 30]`, then the corresponding geometric point ought to be (10, 30). 

But I think it's silly to insist that if I have an image that's stored in the standard scanline order in memory (i.e. row by row) that it's only "appropriate" to index that chunk of memory as `[r, c]` with a C striding convention, rather than `[x, y]` with a Fortran striding convention.

And what if I have an RGB image in scanline order (e.g. rows of pixels as `(r,g,b)` triplets)? In many (but not all!) cases, I'd personally prefer to index it as `[x, y, c]`, which is *neither* Fortran, nor C, but again totally reasonable for a numpy array.

So, I do wish that skimage had made more of an effort up front to be stride-agnostic and coordinate-convention-agnostic. I never run into these problems when using `scipy.ndimage`, which also doesn't need pages of documentation about what various image axes "mean". (This is what I mean when I say that skimage is more opinionated.)

However, at the end of the day, it hardly matters whether I call `convert_to_c_indexing(my_image)` before passing an array to skimage, or whether skimage does that internally.(*) It would have been nice to push the burden of caring about memory layout onto the authors instead of users of the algorithms, but that's not a huge thing. (And maybe that will still be possible in a future version of skimage.)

(*) Where `convert_to_c_indexing()` is some notional function that basically does `np.argsort(arr.strides)` and constructs a new view on the same memory that will allow an algorithm to blindly assume that the last index is fastest-moving.

The code takes the previous winner cells, calls them "candidates", removes the candidates which are already synapsed by this segment, then grows synapses to the candidates randomly, removing the candidate each time.

The way it "removes a candidate" is by swapping it to the end of the list. The Python implementation has the extra optimization of only doing half of a swap -- there's no reason to actually change the value at the end of the list (after `eligibleEnd`).

I'm not sure why we're talking about negative indices, or about duplicates. There are never duplicates in the list of candidates, except for those in the garbage values at the end of the list (due to the extra optimization mentioned above).

The limitation of `resolve_schema ` concerns the constraints. Within a JSON Schema you can create schema dependencies using [`dependencies`](https://spacetelescope.github.io/understanding-json-schema/reference/object.html#schema-dependencies). You could have `allOf` inside those dependencies. Imagine the mess it would be to try to merge them, especially if the schema also contains also [`not`](https://spacetelescope.github.io/understanding-json-schema/reference/combining.html#not) keywords.

The documentation of allOf could list the keywords it supports or doesn't support. I would at least say that `dependencies` are not supported.

>Should they be generated by the server at runtime?

@andfoy, probably, but most important, we need a mapping between the values for the color schemes and the terminal values, that way we can query all available styles (maybe even custom ones!) and generate them on the fly

In my experience, a safer approach is to start a single thread which fetches from a queue and makes the reporting request.  The main thread just adds events to the queue.  This way you don't have a concurrency problem around shared state, and if a lot of events are generated all at once, it gets smoothed out rather than creating a ton of concurrent requests to upstream.

If it happens with all those characters, I think you're looking at the wrong line, because:

```
>>> "\\e".encode("raw-unicode-escape").decode("unicode-escape")
'\\e'
```

(Note that `"\\e"` with a double backslash is correct, because the backslash is an escape character in Python string literals - so e.g. `"\b"` is a one character string containing an ASCII backspace).

Although I added the line, the essential functionality was there before - the `.decode("unicode-escape")` converts a sequence like `\u20ac` into `€`. I'm not sure if I ever knew why that was important (it was >6 years ago!), but I was aiming to add Python 3 support without changing the existing functionality. And if what you show is the problem, it was already there before:

```
# Python 2.7
>>> "\\b".decode("unicode-escape")
u'\x08'
```

The file is in fixed-width format (formatted with tabs), which we don't currently support.
If you ignore the header, then the file can be parsed using `sep=\t` (which fread autodetects), with the exception of a single line 1234 which contains an unquoted `\t` character inside the message (and thus from fread's standpoint it looks like an extra field).

The (x,y) notation in GalSim matches that of ds9, SExtractor, SAOImage, etc.  This is the standard way of referring to positions on an astronomical image.

We do document in the `Image` class docstring that this is backwards of how numpy accesses elements:
```
    The `array` attribute is a NumPy array of the Image's pixels.  The individual elements in the
    array attribute are accessed as `image.array[y,x]`, matching the standard NumPy convention,
    while the Image class's own accessor uses `(x,y)`.
```
We could probably add a bit more explanation of this difference, but I definitely do not want to use the terms `drow` and `dcol` for this.  That IMO would only serve to add to the confusion.  

The API should insulate the user from caring about how x and y are stored internally.  That's why we currently have the above explanation in our description of the `array` attribute, since that's where the user is accessing the implementation, so they need to know.  At other points in the code, (x,y) should be abstractions that the code will implement however is appropriate.

> Its the same argument I would make against using `import numpy as myarrays`.

> For better or for worse, `import astropy.units as u` (or something equivalent) is used widely in the code I have seen, as well as in the astropy docs.

I find it amusing that you think the former import is bad, but the latter is good.  (Or at least acceptable.)  Personally, I hate importing anything `as` anything else.  So I would never advocate `import numpy as myarrays` for the same reason that I don't advocate `import astropy.units as u`.  It's bad enough that `import numpy as np` is so widespread that it has become the almost de facto name of the library, so we're kind of stuck with using that.

The only exception I make as a general rule is `import astropy.io.fits as pyfits`.  I still find it obnoxious that they took a perfectly good package name and bloated the prefix so egregiously.  I think I just keep the `pyfits` name as kind of a protest of their taking over the package.

Mikko,

Thanks for getting back to me, much appreciated. I can tell you the
specific error as it was pretty simple really. The problem was (couple of
times now) that while modifying my REST endpoint code, I introduced bugs
that would cause the code to fail when imported, raising an AttributeError.
This is fine, and expected behavior. The problem is the Resolver catches
the AttributeError and raises it's own with it's own message about "unable
to resolve....". This completely hides the traceback of the code that
generated the AttributeError, making it much more difficult to resolve it.

Because the 'except AttributeError:' in the Resolver doesn't do something
like this 'except AttributeError as e:', putting a breakpoint in the
exception handler doesn't help. And I ran into a situation in production
where a small configuration file change caused the AttributeError, but I
couldn't debug it and we bounced around trying to fix the problem through
inspection.

It 'fixed' the problem by deriving my own Resover class (which I've
attached to this email). It essentially just logs the source exception
before the raise of the ResolveError(msg)

Hope this is helpful, and again, thanks for getting back to me. The
Connexion module is fantastic!!

Doug

I support the idea of having two separate modes. While this approach should be avoided whenever possible as it brings more complexity for both dev and user, I feel that OpenFisca is in a situation where it could really benefit from splitting functionality between two very different cases: “improving the legislation” and “serving the legislation”. This should obviously be a separate issue if we decide to go this way 🙂

As we discussed on the phone, and for the future, I think we should generally let time for code reviews before merging PRs.

This is **especially** true for:

- PRs on openfisca-core. We all rely on this fundamental package, and would gain for its code being high quality. We have to deal with a lot of technical debt today, and non-challenged quick additions are the best way to increase it. I'm not saying this is necessarily the case here, but we should avoid this bad practise in general.
- PRs with breaking changes (major version update). If a change may break some things for users, it is very likely to be worth a discussion with them. It would help everyone to understand the impact, and would allow to challenge the change. 
- To a lesser extend, PR thats add new functionalities (minor version update). Users can have constructive feedback about the new features usages, and implementation.

I understand we all have constraints that push us to want to go fast, and that sometimes production emergency happen. In that case, please consider releasing  your quick fix in a temporary version with pip (3.0.0.dev0 for instance). I don't think we should restrict ourselves to wait for reviews for every single PR, especially if they are about specific things that only impact our respectives scope.

However, for changes that matter, let's please make to sure reach consensus before merging.

Right so the issue is you are putting the whl files directly in the vendor directory. 

From the example walkthrough there is a step where you unzip the wheel files into the vendor directory:
> Download the cryptography wheel file from the Amazon Linux instance and unzip it into the vendor/ directory in the root directory of your Chalice app.

So you would download the file `googleapis-common-protos-somestuffhere.whl` from the Amazon Linux machine you did the build on and UNZIP the contents of that wheel file into the vendor directory. If you look at the example I linked it shows what the vendor directory should look like after unzipping a whl file into it. There should be a directory with the same name as the package, and then another directory with the same name as the package and a `.{version}.dist-info` extension at the end which has some metadata files in it.

`expectThat` isn't actually asserting anything while the test is running, instead they're collected at the end of the method and reported all at the same time. Because code will continue executing after it's been called you can only get the details of the `expectThat` at the end. The test has no conditional logic, instead the logging/reporting metrics need to know which expects passed and which failed.

Here is some [documentation on OAuth grant types](https://oauth.net/2/grant-types/). It looks like your workflow is closest to the "password" grant (which is a really bad idea, since it's exactly the thing OAuth was created to prevent in the first place). I believe you can use the [`LegacyApplicationClient`](https://oauthlib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/oauth2/clients/legacyapplicationclient.html#oauthlib.oauth2.LegacyApplicationClient) class to do this. Try doing something like this:

```python
from oauthlib.oauth2 import LegacyApplicationClient
from requests_oauthlib import OAuth2Session

session = OAuth2Session(client=LegacyApplicationClient())
token = session.fetch_token('https://test..../api/v0.0.2/login', username='secret', password='secret')
```

I'm convinced that it makes perfect sense for these factories to be static methods. The important point is the Python API as seen by a user of the client library. What kind of method it is, in terms of implementation, won't be important to them. The user will expect to be able to write client.metric(...), just as they write client.metric_descriptor(...). It's unreasonable to expect them to think about which factories create objects that know how to talk to the API, and which create glorified structs. That's subject to change anyway. Yet another consideration is how using Client methods takes advantage of the client pattern to reduce the number of names that have to be imported.

Some analysis of each location:

mapreduce/handlers.py#L1084

* This is the `_drop_gracefully` implementation of [`ControllerCallbackHandler`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1053). It will run when the super-class `base_handler.TaskQueueHandler`'s [`initialize()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/base_handler.py#L90) method is called and meets certain conditions (see the code). 

* It sets the `active` property to `False`, then passes the state object to [` ControllerCallbackHandler._finalize_job()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1234). The code trusts the [`_put_state()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1258) method to persist the MapreduceState entity, using `@db.transactional(retries=5)`.

mapreduce/handlers.py#L1178

* This code sets the `active` property of the MapreduceState entity according to whether it has `active_shards`. Perhaps warrants an investigation into whether that can be out-of-sync with reality (one of the shards failing to report back?)

mapreduce/handlers.py#L1391

* This code sets the `active` poperty to `False` if there are no readers available. Probably not the cause of this bug, but worth looking further into. Similarly persists the entity which has just had its `active` property set to `False` using [` ControllerCallbackHandler._finalize_job()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1234).

mapreduce/handlers.py#L1431

* This is `KickOffJobHandler`'s implementation of _drop_gracefully, which is required by [`base_handler.TaskQueueHandler`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/base_handler.py#L59). Once again persists the entity which has just had its `active` property set to `False` using [` ControllerCallbackHandler._finalize_job()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1234).

mapreduce/handlers.py#L1780

* This code represents the creation of a new MapreduceState entity with `active` set to `True`. It's only called from one place: `StartJobHandler`'s [`_start_map()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1701) method. 

mapreduce/api/map_job/map_job_control.py#L203

* This is part of the [`__create_and_save_state()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/api/map_job/map_job_control.py#L189) method of the API class [`Job`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/api/map_job/map_job_control.py#L15). Sets `active` to `True`. The only place this is called from seems to be the `Job`'s method [`submit()`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/api/map_job/map_job_control.py#L115)

It looks like the `NewSquared`object is providing the pickling problem.  It's basically saying that the copy of the class that is being produced is not the same as the class it's looking for.  This is a `dill` pickling issue, that I think is solved -- as you can see if you do some uncommenting and just test `dill.copy(sq)`.  It works, so the object can be pickled.  There's probably some leftover `dill.settings` logic that remains in `pathos.multiprocessing` that needs some cleaning.  I'll have to track that down.  Hmm.

I've provided some alternates, using different pools, which use different backends (and thus different pickling)... and both give the expected answer.  So you could use one of those instead, at least for the time being.

I think this also might push me to expose the `dill.settings` API to `pathos`, to be able to better utilize some of the serialization variants.  It's a pretty common pickling error that one can workaround with either changing `dill.settings` (which needs work to expose it in `pathos`) or by using one of the alternate`pathos.pools`.

Is that a sufficient workaround for you?  If not, I think I'd need to see where `pathos` might need a bug-fix (i.e. it'll take some time, and I might not do it immediately).  I think this is actually a bug, but let me know if the workarounds are sufficient.  If so, you can go forward while I track down the bug.

Sorry this slipped through the cracks somehow and it's taken this long for it to get a reply.

Is this still an issue?  If so, can you post some minimal code that reproduces the error?  Can you reproduce the PicklingError purely from `dill`?  An error of this type from `dill` is common when an object's `__name__ != __qualname__`.  If you can reproduce the error with `dill` (e.g. with no `multiprocess` or `pathos`), then I'd either post this issue with `dill` or OpenSSL, probably the latter.

@amiorin this is likely the result of tilde expansion behavior on your system. pex caches to '~/.pex' (aka the "pex root") by default. in your case, it looks like '~/.pex' is getting expanded as the root users path (/root/.pex) which is likely unreadable after the privilege drop. something between the two forms of sudo is affecting this expansion.

if you can't use a sudo method that affords sane tilde expansion behavior, you can always explicitly control this using the PEX_ROOT env var, e.g.

  `$ PEX_ROOT=/some/readable/path/.pex pex ...`

HTH

I have been able to get by by doing a couple local hacks

        brew install openssl@1.1
        brew install cmake && brew upgrade cmake
        CXX="/usr/bin/c++" OPENSSL_ROOT_DIR="/usr/local/opt/openssl/" ./pants foo.bar

The openssl is to make sure you have the include dir locally.
The cmake command is to upgrade to at least 3.9.5 and pick up the patch for [potential missing symbols](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/commit/087fd3d0e41bd51ca858ed9e93d5f66bf8862a77#diff-2520107225be308c9b77672fb2497213)

The two flags are only needed during the engine bootstrap. The CXX is a workaround for the insufficiently explicit OS X toolchain bug, while the `OPENSSL_ROOT_DIR` will allow it to find the OPENSSL_INCLUDE_DIR and find the missing type_trait include.

> Is it possible to directly use a regular sklearn pipeline with sklearn2pmml?

Not supported at the moment. See https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/issues/3

Very likely, will not be supported in the context of "feature preparation and pre-processing" ever. You should adopt the `DataFrameMapper` approach for that.

> How can I specify the label encoding for PMML?

JPMML-SkLearn supports `LabelBinarizer` and `LabelEncoder` transforms:
```
my_mapper = DataFrameMapper([
  ([<list of my categorical column names>], LabelBinarizer()),
  ('MyTargetFieldName', None)
])
```

> How can I specify split in target / X for PMML?

By JPMML-SkLearn conventions, if you are convertinng a **supervised** estimator object (eg. some classification or regression model), then `DataFrameMapper` rows [0, n_rows - 2] are considered to be active fields, and the last row [n_rows - 1] is considered to be the target field. Also, the target field must be mapped to `None` transform.

If you are converting a **supervised** estimator object (eg. some clustering model), then all `DataFrameMapper` rows [0, n_rows - 1] are considered to be active fields.

> How can I perform the selection of continuous / factor fields? would I need to hard code them?

Currently there is no feature selection support. So, you would need to perform feature selection "manually" in your SkLearn code, and then construct an appropriate `DataFrameMapper` object based on those selection results.

However, built-in support for feature selection seems like a desirable functionality, so perhaps the `sklearn2pmml()` function should take an additional parameter than would allow you to specify some sort of "filtered connection" between the "left-side" `DataFrameMapper` object and the "right-side" estimator object. Something like this:
```Python
def sklearn2pmml(estimator, mapper, mapper_to_estimator_connection = None, pmml)
  pass
```

The responsibility of the `mapper_to_estimator_connection` is to deal with the situation where the number of `DataFrameMapper` output columns is different (typically greater) than the number of estimator input columns. It would would operate on the transformed feature space. For example, it would able to successfully deal with "column expansions" as performed by the `OneHotEncoder` transformer.

In the beginning, it could be a single SkLearn feature selection transformer. Later on, if the concept is successfully validated, it could be a list transformers or even a pipeline of transformers.

to recognize it.

We had a fairly extensive rewrite of the DIDL metadata code, which was started before 0.10 and has continued into 0.11:  see PR #256 

Each DIDL object (DidlMusicArtist, DidlPlaylistContainer) now has a `resources` property which is a *list* of `DidlResource` items. Each of those has a `uri` property, which is probably what you want. Note that in theory it is possible for a DIDL object to have more than one resource, ie. more than one `uri`

So, if t is, e.g., a DidlMusicTrack:
```python
>>>t.resources[0].uri
'x-sonos-http:track%3a3402413.mp3?sid=2&flags=32&sn=4'
```

the coarse levels just do one iteration for a V-cycle, or several for a W-,

is an "exact" solve, so I suppose that would have an inherent tolerance,

This happens when a duplicate (already-announced) route is re-announced. the attributes checks work fine, but the next-hop check causes an unhandled exception due to the aforementioned lack of an `index()` method of `IPSelf`.

I think the fix in the PR is incorrect, but not because of the reason @thomas-mangin stated above:

> Thank you for this patch, but this is not the right solution. change.attributes should not be an IPSelf but an Attributes class.

The reason this isn't correct is that using `.nexthop` directly will cause python to compare the `__repr__()` of the `IPSelf` object, which is always the string `self`. This means a next-hop-self AFI change would not be identified as a route change.

I reckon this should probably be handled as a third case (if `IPSelf`, compare AFI values).

That's a memory error in the _computation graph_, not when loading the data.  Lazy data iteration won't help you here.

There are a few possible causes of this:

1. If your sequences are very long, it's not hard to imagine running out of memory when computing attention.  You could truncate your sequences, if that's an acceptable modeling option for you.
2. Our `Attention` module sacrifices memory usage to gain flexibility in choice of similarity function.  There are much more efficient ways to compute attention, and this piece is probably what's causing your memory errors.  You could change the model code to use a more memory-efficient attention option.
3. This is related to 1, but if you can't truncate your sequences, you could instead decrease your batch size.

Also, you're not actually getting the benefits of a bucket iterator, because you're not providing sorting keys to use to group your data into similarly-sized batches.  You should add a key to your `"iterator"` parameters that's something like `"sorting_keys": [["source_tokens", "num_tokens"], ["target_tokens", "num_tokens"]]`.

Unfortunately it is not easy to assess how "good" a specific choice is, as this remains largely subjective and sometimes motivated by accidental reasons (later repetition of the same pattern, sync w/ left hand, expression etc). I have at hand a good quality edition of fuer elise with a fingering that i'm pretty sure no naive algorithm would ever guess...
Without changing the 'philosophy' of the algo, i see a couple of possible ways to improve it:
- allow the hand to dynamically adapt its rest-position based on the notes already played (i.e. playing e4-e5-e4-e5-e4-e5 would theoretically have zero "difficulty" as the fingers are already in place). At the minute the hand moves rigidly. This is very doable, I already wrote a version of set_fingers_positions_hide() that does that, but didnt have the time to develop it.
- add rules to deal with things like thumb crossing, black keys etc
- having a human pianist judging where the algorithm only miserably fails, and manually tweak the internal parameters to correct for those cases without disrupting what works.

I don't think this or any algorithm for fingering will ever be "great" but I think it would become helpful sometimes to suggest possible solutions one would not think of.

I have now integrated the ability to calculate the standard deviation when calculating the ensemble msd (emsd). 

Previously, I was pretending that each individual particle msd (imsd) has no error and calculating the standard deviation using frequency weights based on the N column, and @caspervdw was concerned that this would underestimate the standard deviation.

Now, in a separate branch (not yet pull-requested here), I have a new approach. I am calculating the imsd standard errors using the Flyvbjerg and Petersen approach, implemented by [pyblock](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyblock) and/or https://github.com/manoharan-lab/flyvbjerg-std-err. Then I can use the imsd stderr to calculate the emsd standard deviation, by using the inverse square of the imsd stderrs as reliability weights. 

I believe that by incorporating the imsd stderr in this way, we are obtaining a better estimate of the std of the emsd.

My branch for calculating the standard deviation is here:
https://github.com/vivarose/trackpy/tree/using_fp_stderr

I have only just arrived at this point so I'm not quite ready to pull-request it. In addition to testing it out a bit, there are a few more things I would like to do:
1) Learn more about _msd_fft() to see if I can calculate the fp_stderr in that function. Right now I can only do it in the _msd_gaps() function.
2) Make it more user-friendly to plot the imsd with error bars. (I have a way of doing it but it's not built-in yet.)

This does not account for errors in the tracking. Those errors are known to increase the msd, especially at small displacements.

May we should see the 'ip_hash' as a load balancing algorithm wt affinity like #3089  rather than using the 'sticky sessison' term. It makes a lot of sense for a client to deal with the same server all through its lifetime, as some of us do have their session data on the server. 

From their docs [here](http://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_upstream_module.html#ip_hash) the ip_hash:
> Specifies that a group should use a load balancing method where requests are distributed between servers based on client IP addresses. The first three octets of the client IPv4 address, or the entire IPv6 address, are used as a hashing key. The method ensures that requests from the same client will always be passed to the same server except when this server is unavailable. In the latter case client requests will be passed to another server. Most probably, it will always be the same server as well.

I believe that the algorithm runs based on the available upstream servers and if i scale containers to say 4, then the upstream is recomputed and the ip_hash also recomputed based on the number of available servers. And there are no session cookies involved here.

However when the container is trashed and new one comes up its ok to terminate and start a new session. Knowing that bringing up new version containers will most likely not be done at a very frequent rate (like say every hour/ 30 mins). Terminating a session even after such period is normal.

Passing an affinity arg on all our request will not be an ideal scenario for us.

I threw in a few `time` blocks to roughly see how long the publish loops were taking. They do indeed seem to take around 3-4 seconds to publish, though I'm not really sure why.

```
+    # while the port is listening, publish to etcd
+    while [[ ! -z $(netstat -lnt | awk "\$6 == \"LISTEN\" && \$4 ~ \".$PORT\" && \$1 ~ \"$PROTO.?\"") ]] ; do
+        START_TIME=$(($(date +%s%N)/1000000))
+        if etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD mk ${ETCD_PATH}/masterLock $HOSTNAME --ttl $ETCD_TTL >/dev/null 2>&1 \
+        || [[ `etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD get ${ETCD_PATH}/masterLock` == "$HOSTNAME" ]] ; then
+            echo "if time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+            etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD set $ETCD_PATH/host $HOST --ttl $ETCD_TTL >/dev/null
+            echo "SET /host time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+            etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD set $ETCD_PATH/port $EXTERNAL_PORT --ttl $ETCD_TTL >/dev/null
+            echo "SET /port time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+            etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD update ${ETCD_PATH}/masterLock $HOSTNAME --ttl $ETCD_TTL >/dev/null
+            echo "UPDATE /masterLock time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+        fi
+        etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD set $HOST_ETCD_PATH/host $HOST --ttl $ETCD_TTL >/dev/null
+        echo "SET /host time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+        etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD set $HOST_ETCD_PATH/port $EXTERNAL_PORT --ttl $ETCD_TTL >/dev/null
+        echo "SET /port time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+        echo "publish loop time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+        sleep $(($ETCD_TTL/2)) # sleep for half the TTL
+        echo "total loop time: $(($(($(date +%s%N)/1000000)) - $START_TIME))"
+    done
```

> Can we add options to get-identities to do whatever its needed for on that command, and remove it as a general option?

I think it applies less importantly on `get-identities` than it does on a lot of other commands.
For example, you can do a retry-safe endpoint deletion with
```
$ globus endpoint delete $EP_ID --map-http-status 404=0
```

> What I don't like about this option is that it causes the REST layer bleed through to the CLI in a big way.

Inevitably, API layer errors need to be propagated up somehow.
Their error codes are an essential piece of information to include, so that layer will have to bubble up somehow. This approach is very direct, and keeps us out of the way of knowledgeable users.

> Can we come up with a standard mapping of HTTP code to exit codes that we use throughout the CLI? ...

I understand that reaction, and it was my original perspective when Karl suggested a handling like this for the Transfer API `code` field. I couldn't follow through on his suggestion because not all of our APIs provide a `code` field, but it inspired this handling for HTTP statuses.

This approach has a few points of superiority over having an explicit mapping of our own.

- It reduces the need for prior knowledge to do correct error handling
- The textual representation of an error also accurately describes how to handle it
- The mapping is always explicit
- Multiple statuses may be mapped to the same exit code
- Mapping a status code back to success is easy, allowing scripts to `set -e` safely without needing `|| true` or other shenanigans

---

My big issue with this option is its overbearing helptext, which seems to also be the basis for this discussion. If we put together some proper docs that include examples, it could be trimmed down signfiicantly, but without that, it needs to be annoyingly noisy in `--help`.
I think we need deeper support in the SDK, so I'm moving this to `1.0`, so as not to block `0.4.2.0`

In an ideal world we'd do all of this enforcement server-side, but pushing it "down the stack" into the SDK seems better than trying to do something specialized in the CLI. I'm in favor of this approach.

Both (a) and (b) are necessary, but independent.

It's always _preferable_, but not necessary, to start with the addition of documentation for the API, as it lets the API maintainers set the expectation on things like date format. That protects us from making mistakes in the SDK docs.

For example, does the API and, by proxy, the SDK accept all of ISO 8601 for `deadline`? Yeah, as Globus team members we can read the API ourselves, but we shouldn't, in principle, have to be conversant in various API codebases.

I'll open an issue to get the ball rolling on (a) and we can add it directly in the SDK if that takes a long time for some reason.

Okay, when you said "hosted" above I mistakenly thought you were referring to the hosted sample data portal that we run and have linked from our web site.

If you installed a copy of the sample data portal repository and are hosting it somewhere on the internet it will not work unless you have registered your own "App" (https://developers.globus.org) and configured it with the correct redirect URI for the domain you have it hosted on. The instructions can be found here: https://github.com/globus/globus-sample-data-portal#create-your-own-app-registration-for-use-in-the-portal

The error message you provided above is "Mismatching redirect URI" which means that Globus Auth is refusing to issue the redirect back to the application because the provided URL has not been registered. It's a security measure. If follow the instructions I linked above it should start working.

Let me know if you have any more trouble.

Sorry, I'm a little late to this "party". Only skimmed over the "strict" channel prio discussion so won't comment on this yet.
But for `featureless_minimization_disabled_feature_flag` you correctly concluded that this doesn't solve the `conda-forge::numpy` de-prioritization because of `conda-forge::blas`'s `track_features`.
From https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6284#issuecomment-408873953:
> An experimental flag `featureless_minimization_disabled_feature_flag` has been added with which the `features` of packages can be completely ignored. This helps with issues like this one that happen on Windows due to packages from `conda-forge` that still have `features: vc`.
Note this **does not** change the behavior around `track_features`, i.e., issues around `conda-forge::numpy` and `conda-forge::blas=*=openblas` still remain until `track_features` is removed from `conda-forge::blas=*=openblas`.

I'm not sure if this is a bug or a feature request, but I put the bug label on it.  

For some context, in conda's earlier days, I think there was an assumption made that it wouldn't be installed next to a system package manager like pacman or yum or apt-get.  There might even be places throughout the code this assumption might even be enforced with hard sys.exit calls (or maybe they've gone away at this point).  It was easier for conda to assume it wasn't at /usr/bin so it wouldn't have to worry about trampling on top of another package manager, or having another package manager trample on top of it.  We might be at the point where we can start to think through how to do better.  I very much want conda to feel exactly like a completely native package manager everywhere its used.

> What about path translation/modification? Can VUnit be instructed to introduce two keywords (prefixes) to make regexp easier?

Path translation is complex. There are a lot of paths everywhere. paths in .tcl files, path given on argv to the simulation. paths given as generics to the test bench from the user. However the recommendation is for the user to create paths in the run.py relative to the project folder or run.py and that the project folder is pointed to relative to the run.py file. Thus running in docker would not need any complex path translation since all paths where created relative to the run.py file.

The way I see it you can already achieve what you want by doing to things.


1. Create `ghdl` in `fakeghdl` containing:
```
docker exec ghdl $ARGS
```

2. Create run.sh containing:
```
export VUNIT_GHDL_PATH=fakeghdl/
setup_docker.sh
python run.py $ARGS
teardown_docker.sh
```

Hi @cfeio.  There are couple of ways to achieve this.

1. The [`--exclude`](http://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/exclude.html) flag.  This would allow you to use an exclusion regex like `^.*anotherindex.*$` to exclude matching indices.
2. The [`--regex`](http://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/regex.html) flag.  This would allow you to more fully define the index expression as `^logstash-\d{4}\d{2}\d{2}$`.  With this you would still need to use `--timestring` if you planned on doing time-based calculations.

Hi @kwill, 

The `ChoicesEnum` inherits from from the `Enum` from the standard library, that is _immutable_. So _afaik_, this is not possible.

Even [extending an `Enum` class that already define members is not possible](https://docs.python.org/3/library/enum.html#restricted-subclassing-of-enumerations).

You can create an dynamic `Enum` from scratch using this, but I don't know if this solves your problem:

``` python
In [1]: from choicesenum import ChoicesEnum
In [2]: Colors = ChoicesEnum('Colors', {'red': '#f00', 'green': '#0f0'})
In [3]: Colors.red
Out[3]: Colors('#f00').red
```

About lambda vs function, there are many discussion about the preference. But the general conclusion is that unless it is used in a very simple form and is fed directly to another function, a function definition should be the choice. Especially in most of the original code, the lambdas were assigned to a variable that essentially created functions. 

In addition, functions are more maintainable and debuggable. When code grows more complex, to expand a function is far easier to the lambda.

Last, the rules are made into the flake8 and other style check tools to suggest against lambda. Unless it is specifically ignored or marked.

> 'asyncio.shield' is not enough, cuz You'r app can be stopped at any moment and shield wouln't be finished

You're right there but I believe those are edge cases, I think the problem proposed here is more focused on an api that is not stopped where this problem still exists when the client disconnects. `asyncio.shield` would be enough there.

Anyway `aiojobs` would suffer from the same problem if `close` is not called before the app exits.

> Well, shield might be enough in many cases. But there is very common pattern: send response quickly and do all slow operations in background.
I believe aiojobs solves it as well as other requirements.

Yeah, I don't argue with the use case about responding quickly and do slow operations in background. It's a necessary and nice tool but in the case you propose I believe `asyncio.shield` would be enough and as a developer I usually aim for keeping my dependencies as low as possible. IMO in summary:

- For client disconnections/fighting against Task cancellation I would recommend `asyncio.shield`. That's why it exists
- In case user wants a way to control tasks in a more granular way, then I would recommend `aiojobs`
- Ofc if user wants to execute background tasks (inside the same loop) I would also recommend `aiojobs`

This is what I believe to be the requirements to fix this issue:

- Properly format all date/time header values to match rfc822.
- Always use the current time for the Date header.
- Either improve performance, or have only a trivial degradation.

These are not requirements, but they should probably be followed anyway:

- not introduce any global variables
- the code should be simpler if at all possible

Write benchmarking code that tests:
- getting an rfc822 formatted time for the Date header
- getting an rfc822 formatted time for the Last-Modified header, passing in a timestamp
- how many requests per second for a GET request that returns Date header
- how many requests per second for a GET request that returns both Date and Last-Modified headers

Indeed, Calico comes with a Java interpreter. However, this is not just a regular system, and might not be what you want (a generic, standard Java system). This Java interpreter is written in Java, but run by .NET/Mono, to integrate with the rest of Calico.

You will need to install Calico for Mac, Windows, or Linux here:
http://calicoproject.org/Calico_Download

Then, you need to make a kernelspec for Java:
`./calico --create-kernelspec --display-name="Calico Java" --nographics --lang=java --only`

That will make the calico_java kernelspec. You can then use it from console/qtconsole, or the notebook:

```
$ ipython console --kernel calico_java
Loading Calico version 4.0.0...
IPython Console 3.1.0
In [1]: x = 1;
In [2]: System.out.println(x);
1
```

Yeah, if you run `gc.disable()`, it won't be able to clean up unused memory, so you eventually need to run garbage collection yourself (`gc.collect()`) or turn it back on (`gc.enable()`).

The question is whether, in the meantime, turning it off eliminates the random slowdowns. If it does, that validates the guess that the slowdowns are due to garbage collection. Or if you run into memory errors before you can see if there's a difference, that also suggests there's a problem with something allocating a lot of memory. Then the problem becomes working out what's doing that.

If you still see the same kind of slowdown with garbage collection disabled, then we'll need to look elsewhere. Though I don't really know where at the moment...

If we're going to cache relations, I'd prefer to do it in a separate collection so that in the common case, the user can just iterate over `endpoint.relations` and `rel.units` and always only see the things that are actually joined.  We could handle the corner case the same as we now do with departing units: in reactive, a relation is "broken" as soon as its last relation is departing (i.e., its `rel.units` collection is empty).

Also note that I didn't end up caching all units, just the ones as they departed.  I determined this ended up being easier because that way I didn't have to deal with ensuring that the relation data cache got updated at a reasonable time, but I actually wonder if it might be better to go ahead and add the caching for everything since that would also allow us to 1) avoid calls to the agent which have been a source of extra, unexpected network traffic to the controller, 2) enforce consistency about when changes to charm state are visible (there's currently some ambiguity with, e.g., config data and possibly relation data because the data that's visible can be different prior to the relevant `-changed` hook triggering, though that's less of an issue when doing reactive "properly").  We technically already have a cache of the config data (in `hookenv.config`), but it's updated on every hook (which is where we first noticed the extra network traffic, always requesting all config during `update-status`); we could change that to only update on `config-changed` and make cached relation data only update on `relation-x-changed`. sudo pfctl -f /dev/stdin
table <forward_subnets> {<B>,!127.0.0.1/8}
rdr pass on lo0 proto tcp to <forward_subnets> -> 127.0.0.1 port 12300
pass out route-to lo0 inet proto tcp to <forward_subnets> keep state

nc -l 12300

# nothing gets routed to nc
```

> Actually, that's a good question. Why *does* FDCapture call SysCapture?
Because sys.stderr may not be a `file` with fileno==2 and *you will want to capture it anyway*.

Aha! The `fdopen` with fileno==6 above is the original `sys.stderr` created by `execnet` (via `xdist`) in the startup code run by the slave process. So I infer that in the "good" invocation, `conftest.py` is run under output capturing, so the StreamHandler stores the fileobj for the redirect; but in the "bad" invocation, `conftest.py` is *not* run under output capturing, so the StreamHandler stores the original fileobj that was in `sys.stderr`

Because of the way `execnet` works, redirecting fileno==2 at the FD level doesn't affect the StreamHandler either way. (And on that note, redirecting fileno==1 at the FD level will break `execnet`'s ability to send output back to the master process for the duration of the output capture. Worrying.)

I've been ruminating on this issue a little.

I think we should probably do the reverse of what I suggested earlier:
* treat test coroutines as pytest-asyncio tests automatically
* treat coroutine fixtures as pytest-asyncio fixtures automatically (which we kinda do now)
* offer an opt-out setting that only treats test coroutines and fixtures with the marker as ours

The rationale is this: simple and common use cases should require a minimum of boilerplate, but more complex use cases should be possible. And code bases using only asyncio are probably a great majority right now.

As for fixtures, you're right. When I initially started writing the fixture code, there was a decorator you had to import and apply to fixtures for the plugin to execute them, but I never got to finish this work. Later this feature was contributed by a different contributor (Alexander Gorokhov), but this work supported only function-scoped fixtures. Function scoped fixtures are actually ok, since in non-asyncio tests you just don't use them, and they only got handled by the plugin if they were executing in the context of an asyncio test.

Then I finished up the feature adding support for other scopes, and now yes, we will conflict with other frameworks. There are also auto-use fixtures to consider.

It'd be nice if we could be intelligent here: for plain function-scoped fixtures we can probably reliably inspect if we're executing for an asyncio test. For module, class and session scoped fixtures we would probably need a marker.

Right. No active unlearning mechanism for V (and D) is implemented, while learning one long axis is very quick thanks to the cumulation. 

One major defect of VD-CMA is that once it learns a wrong long axis (V), it has to first make the vector short and then rotate the vector and make it longer to learn the right long axis. It is problematic when the initial step-size is very small. Then, the evolution path first tends to be long in the negative gradient direction, which is orthogonal to the long axis of the function, and so does V. Therefore VD-CMA needs to wait until V becomes sufficiently short. 

The same happens for CMA (learns a wrong axis at the beginning), but CMA doesn't need to wait this axis becomes short. It learns the right long axis while it makes the wrong axis short. If we have two vectors V in VD covariance model, I guess the situation will be better.

In .NET Core 1 we dropped a lot of dangerous stuff, and, well, customers complained bitterly, so they all came back in 2.0 to enable people to migrate. It's more complicated by the fact that you can use these things securely. Take for example encryption. Encrypting without adding an HMAC is problematic, but we have to expose the crypto primitives because that's what people want, the low level primitives. While there's some scope for static analysis to fix the simple things (don't use MD5, don't use Binary Formatter), there are still legitimate reasons for using both and people then just ignore the false positives, or disable any such analysis.

We can do some things at a higher level, take data protection in asp.net, we force all sorts of opinions on you there and it's secure by default, but because the primitives must be present people are always going to roll their own, and we simply can't stop that. Even sandboxing isn't going to help, we tried that with Code Access Security - but you can't implement it properly within a framework, it needs to be provided by the OS, not something that runs in the same process as your own code.

I know, there is no point in dwelling on what has happened in the past, however, in the daily life of Architects/Developers who were early adopters, the reality is that they have been feeling misguided and in jeopardy of losing traction at work and with the management. The selling point of "cross-platform" is mostly for startups/midsize and green field development, whom historically not have been MS's focus or customer base. Those shops/organizations would still continue to be targeting full framework unless there is a simple and rock solid story for .NET Core migration which seems to be still long ways away. Is there an incentive or compelling reason for them to migrate now or in next one year?

- I'm wondering how important/crucial it is for a developer to know what tooling /SDK/CLI/SharedHost/Runtime versions. I agree that VS 2017 has shown promise and hope, however we already have applications in production based on the project.json (from VS2015) . To upgrade all the servers with relevant bits and migrate to MSBuild/.csproj again takes a lot of convincing and confidence building measures.

In capmq, there are options where you can pass arguments to htslib (-I and -O from https://github.com/mcshane/capmq/blob/master/capmq.c#L288). One way to express the type of this parameter would be to use a map e.g. to describe the inputs from example given in the help text of capmq, you would write the following as an input:
```
htslib_input_options:
  file_format: cram
  lossy_names: true
  seqs_per_slice: 100000
```
where file_format is a special property that is put first in the output array. From the cwl documentation, it looks like the way to implement an map would be to use `type: record` without a field parameter defined and using a valueFrom expression for the inputBinding. You can detect that there was a timeout by checking the length of returned bytes versus the number of bytes requested. It is more like an EOF in Python, rather than a timeout error. That is also how some protocols are using the timeout, as packet delimiter.

An exception would be inconvenient in many cases. E.g. consider reading more than one byte but fewer arrive, the timeout strikes. Then you'd still expect to get the bytes, but how? Attached to the exception? Probably not where most people would expect to find data. When they would be returned with the current ``read()`` call, then the timeout exception would need be raised in the next call which is certainly also not what one would expect.

**Views**: So let me rephrase and see if I understand - you want to create an attribute `ClusterID_1` (for example) and specify that it was derived by clustering on (say) the `PCA` attribute (a 2D array)? In our pipeline that kind of information is implicit, not explicit, and we store it in separate log files. 

But more generally this also boils down to the question of having multiple analysis results. I.e. instead of standardizing on a single `Cluster` attribute for cluster labels, we might want to have multiple such attributes, and subsequent analyses would need to know which one to use.

How about we make standard *naming schemes* in addition to standard *names*? For example, cluster labels could be named `ClusterID` by default, and any additional cluster labels would be named `ClusterID_{something}` (e.g. `ClusterID_ByLouvain`). This will require each method that uses cluster labels to take an argument that specifies which cluster label to use (with default value `Clusters`). I like this idea, as it would also make it possible to adapt to any non-conventional attribute names as well.

**Orientation**: is column access faster? It never occurred to me to check. The matrix is block-compressed so I would have thought it would be symmetric. 

**Normalization:** in our own work, we always recalculate things like normalization and PCA, for every step, and just store raw data and results in the Loom file. This is wasteful of course but simplifies the pipeline. Probably the only representation of normalization that would be easy to implement across pipelines would be to store a full normalized matrix explicitly. I would vote for creating a new file in this case, so that the normalized data can be input to any algorithm, and the algorithm doesn't need to know which specific layer to use. That's also fine. 1.3 million cells from 10X is just 38 GB so it's not terribly inefficient. 

Here more generally also to Alex points above, there is the question of what should be stored in file and what should be stored only transiently in memory. Anything needed for interoperabiity between pipelines would have to be stored in the file. But some things may be better recomputed at each step, because they are closely tied to each pipeline.

**Extensions:** Yes good idea, we should make a HDF5 group for that, e.g. `/extensions/pagoda` etc.

"code above" = "code above the C API", which includes Java code. If you pass a null pointer to a Java method, it may throw a Java null pointer exception well before Z3's C API gets called. So the parameter validation should happen at the Java level. Some parameter validation happens, but it is unlikely complete. A more accurate description is that it is best-effort.

For one example, the andThen tactic just assumes the tactic arrays are non-null tactics. It accesses each element in the array. If the elements are null, then Java throws.
For a different example, Z3Object.arrayToNative checks for null pointers and assumes it is legal to pass null asts in this case. We presume the native Z3 code handles such cases gracefully by throwing or accepting such input when it makes sense.

In summary, I think pull requests to help improve consistency and resilience based on cases where users found it instrumental would be welcome.

Thanks for these cautious notes! So before moving on, we should check whether the pure-Theano CG is competitive with doing the loop in Python.

Regarding the main issue, we found that splitting `cg()` into `cg_step()` and `until_convergence()` won't work. There are some variables that need to be initialized before running CG and are then updated at each step. In the current implementation, these variables are symbolic expressions that are updated as part of the scan. If we move out the update into `cg_step()`, they'd need to become shared variables similar to how `momentum()` etc. initialize some shared variables to be used as accumulators of some sorts. The initial value for these variables depends on the gradient of the loss function, though, they're not simply set to zero. So we'd either need to call `eval()` to get the value to initialize the shared variable with (which would cause a compilation, that's something we should avoid in `lasagne.updates.*`), or initialize it to zero and special-case the very first update (which might slow things down). And even if we got this right, it would make it more difficult to use CG as part of another optimization routine such as HF, which would need to reset things correctly before each CG run.

Long story short: We'll probably be better off not splitting up `cg()`.

> Instinctively I would have constructed the layer theano symbolic output upon call of the layer constructor.
> What is the main use case for the lazy construction of the network ?

The `get_output()` call accepts keyword arguments that can modify the behaviour of the network. The first use case for this was to allow defining a single network architecture to be used with dropout (for training) and without dropout (for testing). For this, we cannot construct the output incrementally on constructing the network, as we need to generate two different expressions later.

If we restricted the API such that every layer creates exactly one expression for training and one expression for testing, we could still construct these two expressions incrementally, but it wouldn't be possible to create even more variants.

> requirement to only do one get_output to share the same random numbers 

See #104 for a discussion of alternative approaches and why we chose this one.

> It is that it's hard to have multiple outputs, it's hard to have multiple inputs, it is hard to do some things which should be one-liners (like an elemwise product layer).

Taking care to have a single `get_output()` call makes multiple-output networks a bit more involved, but what problems do you see for multiple-input networks?

> Harder to debug because it sometime crashes on the get_output line for an error which happens earlier.

This is indeed annoying. We try to catch such errors in the constructor (this usually gives a better description of what went wrong than just creating the symbolic expression and letting Theano fail). If you find errors that are caught too late, we're happy to improve things.

----

Note that you're free to use Lasagne with immediate construction:
```python
input_var = ...
network = InputLayer(input_shape, input_var)
expr = input_var
network = Conv2DLayer(network, ...)
expr = network.get_output_for(expr)
network = Conv2DLayer(network, ...)
expr = network.get_output_for(expr)
network = DenseLayer(network, ...)
expr = network.get_output_for(expr)
```
If you prefer this, you can write a short helper function or a model class that combines adding a layer and updating the expression (or the two expressions if you want to use dropout or batch normalization). You'll still have the benefits of shape propagation, parameter initialization and parameter collection.

Hi @genben, 

I use pyfilesystem in my web-apps, albeit not pyramid. I take a different approach. The FS objects are created when the server is initialized and exist for the lifetime of the server (and closed only when the server exits). 

I close the file object explicitly with my `app_iter` as follows:

```python
def file_chunker(file, size=65536):
    """An iterator that reads a file in chunks."""
    read = file.read
    try:
        chunk = read(size)
        while chunk:
            yield chunk
            chunk = read(size)
    finally:
        file.close()
```

If there is no way around creating the FS object in the view, you could create an `app_iter` that closes the FS object. Something like this...

```python
def file_chunker(parent_fs, file, size=65536):
    """An iterator that reads a file in chunks."""
    read = file.read
    with parent_fs:
        try:
            chunk = read(size)
            while chunk:
                yield chunk
                chunk = read(size)
        finally:
            file.close()
```

Which you would add to your response like this...

```
response.app_iter = file_chunker(my_fs, file)
```

The generator object will hold on to a reference as long as it is iterating, then both the file and FS are explicitly closed.

Hope that helps!

I'm working on analytical application, which requires a lot of straight SQL code and use the DB, which is filled from Django application with django-money inside.

I've faced with situation when I have to calculate total amounts per currency, so I got in the most simple case:
```
# SELECT amount_currency, SUM(amount) from test_currency GROUP BY 1;
 amount_currency | sum
-----------------+-----
 CZK             |  15
 XYZ             |
 USD             |  40
```

It confuses me a bit, because there is nullable amount but some default currency. I think if there is no amount, then it should be really no currency. This default currency could be changed over time, so DB will contain multiple currency values, which should be somehow excluded from aggregation. 
In case with NULL there is always one value to exclude. 

In case if default_currency is something realistic like EUR, then it will slowdown aggregation, unless FILTER clause will be used (PostgreSQL):
```
SELECT amount_currency, SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE amount IS NOT NULL) FROM test_currency GROUP BY 1;
```
It will not be the case if there will be no null values for EUR currency. SUM is simplest case, the more expensive aggregation, the more penalty will be for passing null values to this aggregate.
In some cases null values could be filter out in WHERE clause, but if there are more aggregates (like count of transactions or anything which requires all rows, even with default currency and amount=NULL) it will not work in this way.

Basically it is my motivation to have an ability to set nullable currency column.

I absolutely agree, that some combinations are non-sense, IMO there should be 2 cases:
1) Money - amount and currency
2) No money - no amount and no currency

But now there is no possibility to set currency as NULL :(

For context, the reason we want to set the execute count to "*" is so papermill can inform the user which cell it is executing when it is executing the notebook.         throttle -= 5

        roll -= 100
      elif ord(k) == 67: #right
        roll += 100

      self.vehicle.channels.overrides[3] = throttle
      self.vehicle.channels.overrides[1] = roll
      
      self.log('Throttle: {}, roll: {}', self.vehicle.channels.overrides[3], self.vehicle.channels.overrides[1])
      self._log.flush()

# http://stackoverflow.com/questions/983354/how-do-i-make-python-to-wait-for-a-pressed-key
def read_single_keypress():
  """Waits for a single keypress on stdin.

  This is a silly function to call if you need to do it a lot because it has
  to store stdin's current setup, setup stdin for reading single keystrokes
  then read the single keystroke then revert stdin back after reading the
  keystroke.

  Returns the character of the key that was pressed (zero on
  KeyboardInterrupt which can happen when a signal gets handled)
  """
  fd = sys.stdin.fileno()
  # save old state
  flags_save = fcntl.fcntl(fd, fcntl.F_GETFL)
  attrs_save = termios.tcgetattr(fd)
  # make raw - the way to do this comes from the termios(3) man page.
  attrs = list(attrs_save) # copy the stored version to update
  # iflag
  attrs[0] &= ~(termios.IGNBRK | termios.BRKINT | termios.PARMRK 
                | termios.ISTRIP | termios.INLCR | termios. IGNCR 
                | termios.ICRNL | termios.IXON )
  # oflag
  attrs[1] &= ~termios.OPOST
  # cflag
  attrs[2] &= ~(termios.CSIZE | termios. PARENB)
  attrs[2] |= termios.CS8
  # lflag
  attrs[3] &= ~(termios.ECHONL | termios.ECHO | termios.ICANON
                | termios.ISIG | termios.IEXTEN)
  termios.tcsetattr(fd, termios.TCSANOW, attrs)
  # turn off non-blocking
  fcntl.fcntl(fd, fcntl.F_SETFL, flags_save & ~os.O_NONBLOCK)
  # read a single keystroke
  try:
    ret = sys.stdin.read(1) # returns a single character
  except KeyboardInterrupt: 
    ret = 0
  finally:
    # restore old state
    termios.tcsetattr(fd, termios.TCSAFLUSH, attrs_save)
    fcntl.fcntl(fd, fcntl.F_SETFL, flags_save)
  return ret
```

> Let me know if I got it wrong or there's something missing!

Nothing wrong! But yes some stuff is missing [from the spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7396)

* Only use this behaviour when the specific `Content-Type` is used
* Merge recursively the objects (see a pseudo code example is given in the RFC)

I believe you should move this to `core.utils` and run some of the tests specified in the specs (especially the last one).

I know it would be better if we would have this new behaviour by default, but we can't break existing clients, so we better leave behind the request header `Content-Type: application/merge-patch+json` as the spec says.

Hi @stloma, I can't speak for @leplatrem but I took a few minutes to look through the other tests in the codebase and there seem to be a few in `test_object_permissions.py` that use `sorted()` to compare, probably for exactly this reason. I think that's the best approach. It's not exactly a race condition because it doesn't have to do with two processes happening at the same time, just that we use a `set()` so even within one process, order isn't guaranteed. JSON doesn't have any way to represent a `set()` except as a JSON Array, so it looks like there's an order when we really don't intend for there to be.

Actually, that site was created long ago and I don't believe it is any longer actively maintained.  For instance, there are additional Debauchies (up to `db38`) and Coiflet (up to `coif17`) wavelets available in PyWavelets that do not show up on that site.  We should update the documentation to reflect that.

`demo/plot_wavelets_pyqtgraph.py` is a demo (requiring PyQtGraph) that serves a similar function and plots all of the current discrete wavelets.  The older `demo/plot_wavelets.py` only requires Matplotlib, but does not plot every wavelet in each family.  Those two functions would be very easy to update to use `xs_phi` in the plots for the wavelets.

@dorodnic I see the need for using OpenMP to reduce latency @ HD resolution, but I think there's simply a bug in the usage of OpenMP. Looking at the CPU usage numbers, it looks to me like each thread is replicating the computation of the full frame by itself. It just seems too coincidental to me that each time the thread count is doubled, so is the CPU usage. That or something funky is happening, like using one thread per pixel, so the overhead of adding more threads is outweighing the benefit.

I don't have time to investigate further at the moment, but I agree with @skohlbr that this could probably be fixed with some simple changes to how OpenMP is used.

I believe that the D400 series hardware can't provide a confidence map.

I'm glad to hear you're able to use the custom scalars plugin and like it so far!

Re: the original request, I think for now at least our answer is going to be that the custom scalars plugin is the right tool for this, even if it's a bit heavyweight for the simplest case of plotting two scalar tags on the same chart.  Changing `tf.summary.scalar()` would require a fairly high bar since it's so widely used (even extra optional parameters can possibly lead to user confusion), and it would introduce duplication of functionality with respect to the custom scalars plugin, while ultimately not being able to support the full-featured configuration that the custom scalars plugin provides.

I think if we did want improve the "plot two scalar curves against each other" case, the way we'd likely want to do that is by adding "shortcut" methods for using the custom scalars plugin, e.g. some slightly friendlier API for building the appropriate Layout protocol buffer object, where we could say for example add a `tb.summary.custom_scalar_multiline(title, tag_list)` helper method.  That way it preserves the concept of specifying the tags for a given plot, rather than the plots for a given tag, which matches the way we've structured the custom scalars dashboard on the whole.  But I'll defer to @chihuahua on whether something like that might be appropriate, especially since it might require changes to e.g. merge layouts within a run as well as between runs, etc.

from collections import Counter

from sklearn.metrics import classification_report
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler
from imblearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

X, y = make_classification(
    n_classes=2, class_sep=2, weights=[0.1, 0.9],
    n_informative=3, n_redundant=1, flip_y=0,
    n_features=20, n_clusters_per_class=1,
    n_samples=1000, random_state=10
)

print('Original dataset shape {}'.format(Counter(y)))

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = tts(X, y, random_state=42)

rus = RandomUnderSampler(random_state=42)
knn = KNN()
mms = MinMaxScaler()
pipeline = make_pipeline(rus, mms, knn)

X_trn, X_tst = X_train.copy(), X_test.copy()
y_trn, y_tst = y_train.copy(), y_test.copy()

# manual transformations
X_trn, y_trn = rus.fit_sample(X_trn, y_trn)
X_trn = mms.fit_transform(X_trn)
X_tst = mms.transform(X_tst)
y_hat = knn.fit(X_trn, y_trn).predict(X_tst)
print(classification_report(y_test, y_hat))

X_trn, X_tst = X_train.copy(), X_test.copy()
y_trn, y_tst = y_train.copy(), y_test.copy()

# one liner
y_hat = pipeline.fit(X_trn, y_trn).predict(X_tst)
print(classification_report(y_test, y_hat))
```

Hi all, a bit late to the party.

Re: universal refs vs by-value, I'm personally fine with whichever way it is. Do we have a consensus on whether `Ix&&` should be converted `Ix` in array methods? Note that for literal ints and ints passed by-value the assembly would already be *identical*, you can easily check that. The only difference would be when you pass e.g. a `const size_t&` (why?..) and now it basically has to do a dereference. If we leave it be as universal refs, could do proper forwarding as noted above.

Re: checks, it's probably fine to remove the check from the lowest-level method (e.g. `get_byte_offset()`) as long all higher-level methods remain bound-checked to avoid surprises. Or provide a `get_byte_offset_unchecked()` -- that's a very common idiom e.g. in Rust ([example](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.get_unchecked)).

Running Valgrind on a python executable is always troublesome. CPython, while written in C, doesn't use `malloc`, instead it has its own implementation called `PyMalloc`. When you compile your interpreter you can pass `--with-valgrind` and `--without-pymalloc`, yet this is still not enough.

According to Valgrind, python2 leaks a lot more than python3, but has orders of magnitude fewer memory access errors.
It is possible to suppress Valgrind errors and to even generate suppression rules with `--gen-suppressions`, but once I got a clean Valgrind run on my local machine it didn't work on CI builds.
There's [an outdated valgrind suppression file](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/e42b705188271da108de42b55d9344642170aa2b/Misc/valgrind-python.supp) in the CPython repository too.

All this said, I think there might be a few issues coming from pybind11, not just CPython. For that part take a look at https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/1454, which touches upon the discussion of ridding pybind11 of (known) leaks.

Perhaps some sort of 2.0.0-RC1 release, to let people sticking with stable know it's coming which might prompt them to try it out before the final release (and thus gain some bug reports for unanticipated problems)?
