I can see how this is annoying, however this is exactly how I intended to implement it. There are two paths to choose when implementing this:

* Execute the command in the user's shell. That means that you'd write `["command", "gpg -d --use-agent foo"]`, which gives you the benefits like path expansion (like in your example), ability to reference environment variables, etc. However, it also means that you will have to take care of escaping/quoting spaces like you do in the shell, which can be annoying.
* Don't execute the command in a shell

I don't think behaving *partially* like a shell, i.e. expanding `~` but still treating spaces literally, is a good idea here, so I chose the latter option because it seemed like the stricter (and simpler) one.

What I usually recommend in this situation is to write `["command", "sh", "-c", "gpg -d --use-agent foo"]`. I think this should be documented better.

I'm using Ubuntu 16.04. Certbot 0.19.0. I think the biggest thing that's needed here is documentation. The Ubuntu package installs a systemd timer (I'm 100% sure of that because I didn't know systemd timers existed until I happened across a post that mentioned a cerbot systemd timer, I think the script may have been there but I had to modify it to add the renew hook arguments). I'm also reasonably confident that the package also installed a cron job in `/etc/cron.d`, but it was a while ago that I set it up so it is possible that I put that there following some instructions. It's also possible that it was left there as a remnant of the certbot package switching at some point. But if you read the instructions about auto renewal for Ubuntu 16.04, it says this:

> The Certbot packages on your system come with a cron job that will renew your certificates automatically before they expire.

Furthermore, the certbot documentation on renewals, [here](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewal), makes no mention of systemd, only cron.

So I would say the following would go along way to solving this issue:

1. Update the [certbot installation documentation](https://certbot.eff.org/all-instructions/#ubuntu-16-04-xenial-nginx) for Ubuntu to correctly document that certbot installs with a systemd timer, rather than a cron job.
2. Update the [certbot usage documentation](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewal) to mention that some packages setup a systemd script.

Furthermore you should probably check if I'm right that the Ubuntu certbot packages are installing both a cron job and a systemd timer, because if it is both, that's a bug.

A final thing that would help I think is example hooks along with the installation. It's currently really hard to know what the "right" way to install hooks is, the documentation both mentions `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks` as well as `--deploy-hook`. Most people just want to restart their webserver as a hook, so including some examples for each webserver that can be copied into the `renewal-hooks` directory would help a lot.

To flesh out my explanation above with an example, imagine you have to renew `standalone.example.org` and `webroot.example.org` where each domain uses plugin included in the domain. You could write a renew hook like:
```
if [ "$RENEWED_DOMAINS" = "webroot.example.org" ];
  service lighttpd start
else
  service lighttpd stop
fi
```
Using this script, the sequence of events is:
1. User runs `certbot renew`
2. Domain A is attempted to be renewed
3. Renew hook is run with domain A
4. Domain B is attempted to be renewed
5. Renew hook is run with domain B

You have no way of knowing in advance whether domain A is "webroot.example.org" or "standalone.example.org".

What's the problem with this? Since domain A is attempted to be renewed before your hook is run, you don't have a chance to start or stop your server. Similarly, you can't do this before running Certbot because you don't know which domain will be attempted to renewed first.

Personally, I'm not very worried about this issue until I see a real use case for it. Why can't you use the same plugin for both domains? I believe you'd have the same problem with standalone and the Apache/Nginx plugins as well (unless you've configured Apache/Nginx to not handle unencrypted HTTP traffic and standalone to do the HTTP challenge).

Hey @sdtsui. The checked off items check for a different thing thanthe non-checked off ones.They check that there is code in the deployed address, while the non-checked off items specify that they should wait for the corresponding event to be emitted and seen.

This would need some changes in the corresponding rpc client functions. For example [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/459c13e742e84441e7f3a6dab2fe46b4d9d8b6e8/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L1091) is where the `settle()` function would need to be checked.

You would need to make sure that the blockchain event gets emitted. So you would need to use the proper rpc calls to listen for the correct event and the correct arguments.

To detect replay attacks across forks we would need to have a fork Oracle, just like with ETH/ETC fork oracles. We can't know in advance about hard forks so this is something we would need to be solving in a case by case basis. We can have a connection with a fork oracle from the very beginning of the contract deployment and just have control of the Oracle that can update the contracts in case of a fork.

@gravyboat 
A quality range selection via the normal quality list would be a nice-to-have feature, but you can already filter out unwanted qualities and select the `best` one out of the remaining ones while still having a fallback, [like I've described above in the linked comment](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink-twitch-gui/issues/434#issuecomment-291055484). If desired, different config files containing these parameters can be set up, so that loading a specific config file becomes the quality selection.

```bash
# "source"
streamlink --default-stream "best" twitch.tv/CHANNEL
# "high"
streamlink --default-stream "high,best" --stream-sorting-excludes ">720p30,<540p" twitch.tv/CHANNEL
# "medium"
streamlink --default-stream "medium,best" --stream-sorting-excludes ">540p30,<480p" twitch.tv/CHANNEL
# "low"
streamlink --default-stream "low,best" --stream-sorting-excludes ">360p30" twitch.tv/CHANNEL
# "audio"
streamlink --default-stream "audio,audio_only" twitch.tv/CHANNEL
```

If you want to add a new parameter, being able to set aliases for parsable qualities and quality ranges would make sense, so that those can be whitelisted instead of blacklisted (`--stream-sorting-excludes`). Something like this would be easier to understand, I think:
```bash
# multiple alias-params could be allowed here
streamlink --stream-alias "high_alias:<=720p30,>=540p" twitch.tv/CHANNEL high,high_alias
```
Without adding a new parameter (or just using the `--default-stream` param), this would require a new quality selection format, since commas can't be used for both boolean ORs and ANDs at the same time:
```bash
# how to interpret the commas here?
streamlink twitch.tv/CHANNEL high,<=720p30,>=540p
# this format would require quotation marks and would not be backwards compatible
streamlink twitch.tv/CHANNEL "high||<=720p30&&>=540p"
```

This happens because of a floating point number issue, and I think the jqueryui slider we are using handles it incorrectly. When a slider's max is set, it's modified by this function: https://github.com/jquery/jquery-ui/blob/89af4c292eaa5fc1c83437ca71085264dcbef34a/ui/widgets/slider.js#L551

In that function, jqueryui tries to address roundoff error from the step by calculating what the max should be if we take the given number of steps from the min (let's call this the stepmax). However, it subtracts a whole step if the stepmax is more than the max. I think what it should do is realize that the last step may not be a full step, and set the max to what was explicitly set.

For now, a workaround is to set max to something like `0.601`.

Perhaps this is another reason for us to move off of the jqueryui slider: #630.

I'm less worried about the python generator (which as you say, is a no-op).

What we should get agreement on is:

1. Is onnx.proto.in a valid proto file, at least syntactically?   As someone who edits the file, I'd prefer that my text editor's language services don't break if we can achieve that.

2. For conditional inclusion of specific declarations (e.g., messages, fields, etc), I think we can use an embedded-in-comments ifdef syntax. Honestly, that will keep us honest wrt colliding field IDs.  We should decide whether we want #if or #iddef semantics. For our purposes, I'd strongly favor #ifdef semantics.

3. I don't have a good answer for how to deal with the optional keyword. If we are going to treat proto2-vs-proto3 as a first-class thing, I assume we could simply bulk inject it (or bulk remove it).  I'm loath to add a general #define.

For windows, in ACTIVATE_BAT you can replace:

```
set VIRTUAL_ENV=__VIRTUAL_ENV__
```

with:

```
pushd %~dp0..
set VIRTUAL_ENV=%CD%
popd
```

This is better than just setting it to `%~dp0..` directly in that you end up with the same absolute path you would without the change, but it's relocatable.
